# Metro Integrated Planning System (MMS)

## Project Overview
High-Performance Enterprise Integrated Planning System with "Semantic Zoom" UX (Google Earth-style) for metro fleet management. Renders 50+ trains and 10 years of tasks at constant 60fps using WebGL.

---

## HARNESS: Fundamental Concept (READ THIS FIRST)

> **This project uses a Harness.** If you don't know what that is, STOP and research:
> - [Effective harnesses for long-running agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents)

### What is a Harness?

A **Harness** is a framework that enables AI agents to work on long tasks across multiple context windows/sessions.

**Problem:** Each new Claude Code session starts with NO memory of previous sessions.

**Solution:** State files persist context between sessions:
- `claude-progress.txt` → session history
- `features.json` → requirements with status (passes: true/false)

### Work Protocol

```
1. READ claude-progress.txt (understand current state)
2. READ features.json (find next feature)
3. IMPLEMENT one feature
4. TEST visually (Playwright MCP)
5. COMMIT after passing
6. UPDATE claude-progress.txt
7. STOP (leave codebase production-ready)
```

### Self-Correction Protocol

If you don't understand what harness is after reading this:
1. **SEARCH:** WebSearch "Anthropic harness agents 2025"
2. **ANALYZE:** Why was local documentation unclear?
3. **FIX:** Update CLAUDE.md or hooks to improve clarity
4. **DOCUMENT:** Record the fix in claude-progress.txt

### Harness Files

| File                  | Purpose              | When to Read       |
|-----------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| `claude-progress.txt` | Session history      | **ALWAYS first**   |
| `features.json`       | Features with status | After progress.txt |
| `CLAUDE.md`           | Project rules        | Reference          |

---

## KISS Philosophy

**KISS is NOT "do the minimum". It IS "don't add complexity that blocks progress".**

The goal is **speed of delivery with quality**. The product must impress the client to win contracts.

### What KISS means (examples)

| ✅ DO                                                         | ❌ DON'T                                                          |
|--------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Add polish, debug tools, UX refinements that accelerate work | Spend days on infrastructure before having something presentable |
| Use best practices that prevent future problems              | Add abstractions for hypothetical future needs                   |
| Preserve knowledge/tips for when they become relevant        | Ignore proven patterns just because "not needed yet"             |

### When to Increase Complexity (examples)

| ✅ Increase complexity when...     | ❌ Do NOT increase when... |
|-----------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Current approach blocks a feature | "It would be cleaner"     |
| Performance is unacceptable       | "Best practices say so"   |
| Code duplication becomes painful  | Premature optimization    |
| Team needs shared abstractions    | Speculative future needs  |

### Example: POC-first approach

When the monorepo setup consumed days debugging config, a pure HTML POC (`design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html`) was created to validate the concept quickly.

**Lesson:** Start simple, validate fast, then add complexity incrementally.

---

## Production App: apps/canvas

The production application is located at `apps/canvas/` - a React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS 6 app that implements the "Semantic Zoom" UX concept.

### Product Entry Point

**`apps/canvas`** - The production React app with semantic zoom.

```bash
# Run development server (http://localhost:5173)
cd apps/canvas && bun run dev

# Or from root
bun run dev --filter=@mms/canvas
```

### Tech Stack

| Technology    | Version | Purpose                                   |
|---------------|---------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Vite**      | 5.x     | Build tool and dev server                 |
| **React**     | 18.3.1  | UI framework (required for BlueprintJS 6) |
| **BlueprintJS** | 6.1.0 | UI component library                      |
| **R3F**       | 8.x     | React Three Fiber for WebGL               |
| **drei**      | 9.x     | R3F helpers and components                |
| **Three.js**  | 0.170.x | 3D rendering engine                       |

### Features

| Feature       | Description                                         | LOD Level |
|---------------|-----------------------------------------------------|-----------|
| **Pan**       | Drag to navigate through time (X) and trains (Y)    | All       |
| **Zoom**      | Scroll to zoom in/out, zoom centers on cursor       | All       |
| **Macro View** | Heatmap showing task density (yellow→orange→red)   | < 0.5x    |
| **Meso View** | DNA-like task strips with colored blocks            | 0.5x - 2x |
| **Micro View** | Detailed task cards with labels and grid           | > 2x      |
| **Sidebar**   | Train IDs synced with vertical pan (BlueprintJS Card) | All     |
| **Timeline**  | Date labels with adaptive granularity               | All       |

### Roadmap

See `features.json` for complete feature list. Key phases:

```
✅ F001-F015:  POC validation (archived)
🔄 F101-F103:  Foundation - Vite + React 18 + BlueprintJS + R3F ← CURRENT
⏳ F104-F107:  Core rendering - Data, MesoLayer, pan/zoom, LOD
⏳ F108-F110:  All LOD layers - Macro, Micro, transitions
⏳ F111-F114:  UI integration - Sidebar, timeline, controls
⏳ F115-F117:  Polish - Instructions, performance, visual parity
```

---

## POC Reference

A standalone HTML demo exists for visual and algorithmic reference:

**`design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html`** - Validated prototype.

```bash
# View the POC reference
open design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html    # macOS
```

Use as reference for:
- Visual appearance of semantic zoom
- LOD algorithms (Macro/Meso/Micro)
- Rendering logic (heatmap, task blocks, cards)

---

### Key Rule

**Do NOT write throwaway code without clear time/agility gains.** If code will be rewritten anyway, the simple version must provide faster iteration or validation—otherwise, just build it right the first time.
**If you know something will cause problems later (performance, UX, maintainability), address it now based on knowledge and testing—not fear, not speculation.**

---

## Documentation Rules

- **Language:** Always write documentation in English
- **Location:** Prefer updating existing docs over creating new files
- **Conciseness:** Keep docs actionable, not verbose

---

## Tech Stack

### Production App (apps/canvas)
- **Build Tool:** Vite 5.x
- **Frontend:** React 18.3.1 (required for BlueprintJS 6)
- **UI Components:** BlueprintJS 6.1.0 (Palantir)
- **Graphics Engine:** @react-three/fiber 8.x, @react-three/drei 9.x, three 0.170.x
- **State Management:** Custom state-machine (packages/toolkit)

### Monorepo Infrastructure
- **Runtime:** Bun 1.2.0
- **Monorepo:** Turborepo
- **Shared Package:** @mms/toolkit (React 18 || 19 compatible)

## Quick Commands

> ⚠️ **ALWAYS run `./init.sh --no-server` first** to verify environment.

### Production App (apps/canvas)

```bash
# 1. Initialize and verify environment
./init.sh --no-server

# 2. Install dependencies
bun i

# 3. Run production app (http://localhost:5173)
cd apps/canvas && bun run dev

# 4. Build for production
cd apps/canvas && bun run build

# 5. Type check
bun run typecheck
```

### Archived POC

```bash
# View the archived POC (reference only)
open URGENT_POC/canvas.html
```

### Troubleshooting

This is a **monorepo**. If root scripts fail, run directly in the app:

```bash
# Direct command (always works)
cd apps/canvas && bun run dev

# Check what scripts exist
cat apps/canvas/package.json | grep -A10 '"scripts"'
```

---

## CRITICAL AI RULES

> **READ THIS FIRST.** These rules address fundamental AI limitations.

### Rule 1: CHECK THE CURRENT DATE

**Your training data is outdated.** Before starting ANY work:

```bash
date  # Check actual current date
```

**DO NOT assume the current year is your training year.** If your training was in 2024 but it's now 2025:
- Documentation has changed
- Library versions have changed
- APIs have changed
- Best practices have evolved

**Always include the CURRENT YEAR in documentation searches.**

### Rule 2: YOU CANNOT SEE THE UI

**You are blind to rendered output.** You MUST use MCP visual testing.

```
What you know:              Reality:
✓ Code compiled             ✗ Blank white screen
✓ No TypeScript errors      ✗ React error boundary
✓ Tests passed              ✗ Component doesn't render
```

**NEVER mark a UI feature as complete without MCP screenshot verification.**

### Rule 3: INSTALL DEPENDENCIES VIA COMMANDS

**NEVER manually edit dependency files to add packages.**

```bash
# CORRECT
bun add react
bun add @react-three/fiber

# WRONG - DO NOT DO THIS
# Manually editing package.json: "react": "^18.0.0"
```

Why: Package managers resolve correct versions and peer dependencies.

### Rule 4: RESEARCH BEFORE GUESSING

**When stuck, STOP and research.** Don't try random solutions.

See the STOP-SEARCH-UNDERSTAND-APPLY method below.

### Rule 5: SEARCH FOR UPDATED DOCUMENTATION

**Your training data is stale.** For complex configurations:

```bash
# Always search with current year
WebSearch: "[technology] [topic] documentation 2025"
WebFetch: [official docs URL]
```

**Do NOT rely on training data for:**
- Library configurations
- API patterns
- Framework setup
- Plugin systems

### Rule 6: VERIFY COMMANDS BEFORE RUNNING

**NEVER assume a command will work.** Before running ANY command:

```bash
# WRONG - Running blindly
bun run dev  # ← May fail if monorepo filter is wrong

# CORRECT - Verify first
cat package.json | grep -A10 '"scripts"'  # Check what scripts exist
./init.sh --no-server                      # Verify environment
bun run dev                                 # Then run
```

**If a command fails:**
1. **READ the error message** - Don't retry blindly
2. **Check the configuration** - Is the command correct for this project?
3. **Try the direct approach** - `cd apps/system && bun run dev`
4. **Document the solution** - Update relevant docs so future sessions don't repeat the error

**Common monorepo gotchas:**
- Root `package.json` scripts may use filters that don't match package names
- Always check `apps/*/package.json` for the actual package name
- When in doubt, run commands directly in the package folder

---

## MCP VISUAL TESTING (MANDATORY)

### Why This is Required

You cannot see the app you're building. Without visual testing, you will:
- Mark blank screens as "working"
- Miss CSS/layout issues
- Overlook runtime errors visible only in UI

### Testing Protocol: Playwright MCP

Use **Playwright MCP** for visual tests. Runs headless and **does not steal focus** from the user's window.

#### Step 1: Navigate to the page

```
mcp__playwright__browser_navigate url="file:///full/path/to/file.html"
```

Or for local server:
```
mcp__playwright__browser_navigate url="http://localhost:4321"
```

#### Step 2: Check console (JS errors)

```
mcp__playwright__browser_console_messages level="error"
```

- Red errors → **FAIL** (investigate before continuing)
- Yellow warnings → Evaluate (e.g., "Tailwind CDN production warning" is OK)

#### Step 3: Capture screenshot

```
mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot filename="screenshots/test-name.png"
```

For scrollable pages:
```
mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot fullPage=true filename="screenshots/test-full.png"
```

#### Step 4: Analyze visually

Check in the screenshot:
- [ ] Content visible? (not blank/empty)
- [ ] Layout correct? (elements positioned as expected)
- [ ] Colors correct? (matching design)
- [ ] No visual artifacts?

#### Step 5: Interactions (if needed)

```
mcp__playwright__browser_click element="button X" ref="e123"
mcp__playwright__browser_type element="field Y" ref="e456" text="value"
```

#### Step 6: Close browser at the end

```
mcp__playwright__browser_close
```

### Approval Criteria

| Criterion | PASS | FAIL |
|-----------|------|------|
| Console without errors | ✅ | ❌ Red errors |
| Content rendered | ✅ | ❌ Blank/empty screen |
| Layout correct | ✅ | ❌ Misaligned elements |
| JS executed | ✅ | ❌ Dynamic content missing |

### Final Decision

```
Visual OK + Console clean    → passes: true
Visual OK + Console warnings → Evaluate (may pass)
Visual OK + Console errors   → FAIL (fix it)
Visual broken                → FAIL (fix it)
```

### Playwright MCP Tools

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `mcp__playwright__browser_navigate` | Open page (file:// or http://) |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot` | Capture viewport or fullPage |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_snapshot` | Accessibility tree (debug) |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_console_messages` | View JS errors/warnings |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_click` | Click on element |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_type` | Type in field |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_close` | Close browser |


---

## ERROR HANDLING: STOP-SEARCH-UNDERSTAND-APPLY

2. SEARCH

---

## HARNESS: Fundamental Concept (READ THIS FIRST)

### Work Protocol

```

```

### Self-Correction Protocol

### Harness Files

The goal is **speed of delivery with quality**. The product must impress the client to win contracts.

|--------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Add polish, debug tools, UX refinements that accelerate work | Spend days on infrastructure before having something presentable |
| Use best practices that prevent future problems              | Add abstractions for hypothetical future needs                   |
| Preserve knowledge/tips for when they become relevant        | Ignore proven patterns just because "not needed yet"             |

|-----------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Current approach blocks a feature | "It would be cleaner"     |
| Performance is unacceptable       | "Best practices say so"   |
| Code duplication becomes painful  | Premature optimization    |
| Team needs shared abstractions    | Speculative future needs  |

### Example: POC-first approach

When the monorepo setup consumed days debugging config, a pure HTML POC (`design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html`) was created to validate the concept quickly.

**Lesson:** Start simple, validate fast, then add complexity incrementally.

## CRITICAL AI RULES

> **READ THIS FIRST.** These rules address fundamental AI limitations.

### Rule 1: CHECK THE CURRENT DATE

**Your training data is outdated.** Before starting ANY work:

```bash
date  # Check actual current date
```

**DO NOT assume the current year is your training year.** If your training was in 2024 but it's now 2025:
- Documentation has changed
- Library versions have changed
- APIs have changed
- Best practices have evolved

**Always include the CURRENT YEAR in documentation searches.**

### Rule 2: YOU CANNOT SEE THE UI

**You are blind to rendered output.** You MUST use MCP visual testing.

```
What you know:              Reality:
✓ Code compiled             ✗ Blank white screen
✓ No TypeScript errors      ✗ React error boundary
✓ Tests passed              ✗ Component doesn't render
```

**NEVER mark a UI feature as complete without MCP screenshot verification.**

### Rule 3: INSTALL DEPENDENCIES VIA COMMANDS

**NEVER manually edit dependency files to add packages.**

```bash
# CORRECT
bun add react
bun add @react-three/fiber

# WRONG - DO NOT DO THIS
# Manually editing package.json: "react": "^18.0.0"
```

Why: Package managers resolve correct versions and peer dependencies.

### Rule 4: RESEARCH BEFORE GUESSING

**When stuck, STOP and research.** Don't try random solutions.

See the STOP-SEARCH-UNDERSTAND-APPLY method below.

### Rule 5: SEARCH FOR UPDATED DOCUMENTATION

**Your training data is stale.** For complex configurations:

```bash
# Always search with current year
WebSearch: "[technology] [topic] documentation 2025"
WebFetch: [official docs URL]
```

**Do NOT rely on training data for:**
- Library configurations
- API patterns
- Framework setup
- Plugin systems

### Rule 6: VERIFY COMMANDS BEFORE RUNNING

**NEVER assume a command will work.** Before running ANY command:

```bash
# WRONG - Running blindly
bun run dev  # ← May fail if monorepo filter is wrong

# CORRECT - Verify first
cat package.json | grep -A10 '"scripts"'  # Check what scripts exist
./init.sh --no-server                      # Verify environment
bun run dev                                 # Then run
```

**If a command fails:**
1. **READ the error message** - Don't retry blindly
2. **Check the configuration** - Is the command correct for this project?
3. **Try the direct approach** - `cd apps/system && bun run dev`
4. **Document the solution** - Update relevant docs so future sessions don't repeat the error

**Common monorepo gotchas:**
- Root `package.json` scripts may use filters that don't match package names
- Always check `apps/*/package.json` for the actual package name
- When in doubt, run commands directly in the package folder

---

## MCP VISUAL TESTING (MANDATORY)

### Why This is Required

You cannot see the app you're building. Without visual testing, you will:
- Mark blank screens as "working"
- Miss CSS/layout issues
- Overlook runtime errors visible only in UI

### Testing Protocol: Playwright MCP

Use **Playwright MCP** for visual tests. Runs headless and **does not steal focus** from the user's window.

#### Step 1: Navigate to the page

```
mcp__playwright__browser_navigate url="file:///full/path/to/file.html"
```

Or for local server:
```
mcp__playwright__browser_navigate url="http://localhost:4321"
```

#### Step 2: Check console (JS errors)

```
mcp__playwright__browser_console_messages level="error"
```

- Red errors → **FAIL** (investigate before continuing)
- Yellow warnings → Evaluate (e.g., "Tailwind CDN production warning" is OK)

#### Step 3: Capture screenshot

```
mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot filename="screenshots/test-name.png"
```

For scrollable pages:
```
mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot fullPage=true filename="screenshots/test-full.png"
```

#### Step 4: Analyze visually

Check in the screenshot:
- [ ] Content visible? (not blank/empty)
- [ ] Layout correct? (elements positioned as expected)
- [ ] Colors correct? (matching design)
- [ ] No visual artifacts?

#### Step 5: Interactions (if needed)

```
mcp__playwright__browser_click element="button X" ref="e123"
mcp__playwright__browser_type element="field Y" ref="e456" text="value"
```

#### Step 6: Close browser at the end

```
mcp__playwright__browser_close
```

### Approval Criteria

| Criterion | PASS | FAIL |
|-----------|------|------|
| Console without errors | ✅ | ❌ Red errors |
| Content rendered | ✅ | ❌ Blank/empty screen |
| Layout correct | ✅ | ❌ Misaligned elements |
| JS executed | ✅ | ❌ Dynamic content missing |

### Final Decision

```
Visual OK + Console clean    → passes: true
Visual OK + Console warnings → Evaluate (may pass)
Visual OK + Console errors   → FAIL (fix it)
Visual broken                → FAIL (fix it)
```

### Playwright MCP Tools

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `mcp__playwright__browser_navigate` | Open page (file:// or http://) |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot` | Capture viewport or fullPage |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_snapshot` | Accessibility tree (debug) |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_console_messages` | View JS errors/warnings |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_click` | Click on element |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_type` | Type in field |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_close` | Close browser |


---

## ERROR HANDLING: STOP-SEARCH-UNDERSTAND-APPLY

### Classify the Error First

| Error Type | Action |
|------------|--------|
| Typo, syntax, obvious | Fix directly |
| Config, runtime, library, unknown | **RESEARCH FIRST** |

### The Method

```
1. STOP
   - Recognize you're stuck
   - Do NOT try random solutions

2. SEARCH
   - WebSearch: "[error] + [tech] + [CURRENT YEAR]"
   - WebFetch: Official documentation
   - Include current year, not training year

3. UNDERSTAND
   - Read results carefully
   - Identify ROOT CAUSE
   - Know WHY before fixing

4. APPLY
   - Implement correct solution
   - From documentation, not guessing

5. DOCUMENT
   - Update relevant docs if this is a recurring issue
```

## LOD System (Level of Detail)

The system implements three rendering layers based on camera zoom:

- **Macro (zoom < 0.5):** Procedural shader heatmap - 1 draw call
- **Meso (0.5 ≤ zoom ≤ 2.0):** InstancedMesh with 60k instances
- **Micro (zoom > 2.0):** HTML overlay with Line2 dependency curves

## Current State

Read `claude-progress.txt` for session history and `features.json` for requirements status.

# Metro Integrated Planning System (MMS)

## Project Overview
High-Performance Enterprise Integrated Planning System with "Semantic Zoom" UX (Google Earth-style) for metro fleet management. Renders 50+ trains and 10 years of tasks at constant 60fps using WebGL.

---

## HARNESS: Fundamental Concept (READ THIS FIRST)

> **This project uses a Harness.** If you don't know what that is, STOP and research:
> - [Effective harnesses for long-running agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents)

### What is a Harness?

A **Harness** is a framework that enables AI agents to work on long tasks across multiple context windows/sessions.

**Problem:** Each new Claude Code session starts with NO memory of previous sessions.

**Solution:** State files persist context between sessions:
- `claude-progress.txt` → session history
- `features.json` → requirements with status (passes: true/false)

### Work Protocol

```
1. READ claude-progress.txt (understand current state)
2. READ features.json (find next feature)
3. IMPLEMENT one feature
4. TEST visually (Playwright MCP)
5. COMMIT after passing
6. UPDATE claude-progress.txt
7. STOP (leave codebase production-ready)
```

### Self-Correction Protocol

If you don't understand what harness is after reading this:
1. **SEARCH:** WebSearch "Anthropic harness agents 2025"
2. **ANALYZE:** Why was local documentation unclear?
3. **FIX:** Update CLAUDE.md or hooks to improve clarity
4. **DOCUMENT:** Record the fix in claude-progress.txt

### Harness Files

| File                  | Purpose              | When to Read       |
|-----------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| `claude-progress.txt` | Session history      | **ALWAYS first**   |
| `features.json`       | Features with status | After progress.txt |
| `CLAUDE.md`           | Project rules        | Reference          |

---

#### Step 1: Navigate to the page

```
mcp__playwright__browser_navigate url="file:///full/path/to/file.html"
```

Or for local server:
```
mcp__playwright__browser_navigate url="http://localhost:4321"
```

#### Step 2: Check console (JS errors)

```
mcp__playwright__browser_console_messages level="error"
```

- Red errors → **FAIL** (investigate before continuing)
- Yellow warnings → Evaluate (e.g., "Tailwind CDN production warning" is OK)

#### Step 3: Capture screenshot

```
mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot filename="screenshots/test-name.png"
```

For scrollable pages:
```
mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot fullPage=true filename="screenshots/test-full.png"
```

#### Step 4: Analyze visually

Check in the screenshot:
- [ ] Content visible? (not blank/empty)
- [ ] Layout correct? (elements positioned as expected)
- [ ] Colors correct? (matching design)
- [ ] No visual artifacts?

#### Step 5: Interactions (if needed)

```
mcp__playwright__browser_click element="button X" ref="e123"
mcp__playwright__browser_type element="field Y" ref="e456" text="value"
```

#### Step 6: Close browser at the end

```
mcp__playwright__browser_close
```

### Approval Criteria

| Criterion | PASS | FAIL |
|-----------|------|------|
| Console without errors | ✅ | ❌ Red errors |
| Content rendered | ✅ | ❌ Blank/empty screen |
| Layout correct | ✅ | ❌ Misaligned elements |
| JS executed | ✅ | ❌ Dynamic content missing |

### Final Decision

```
Visual OK + Console clean    → passes: true
Visual OK + Console warnings → Evaluate (may pass)
Visual OK + Console errors   → FAIL (fix it)
Visual broken                → FAIL (fix it)
```

### Playwright MCP Tools

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `mcp__playwright__browser_navigate` | Open page (file:// or http://) |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot` | Capture viewport or fullPage |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_snapshot` | Accessibility tree (debug) |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_console_messages` | View JS errors/warnings |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_click` | Click on element |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_type` | Type in field |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_close` | Close browser |


---

## ERROR HANDLING: STOP-SEARCH-UNDERSTAND-APPLY

### Classify the Error First

| Error Type | Action |
|------------|--------|
| Typo, syntax, obvious | Fix directly |
| Config, runtime, library, unknown | **RESEARCH FIRST** |

### The Method

```
1. STOP
   - Recognize you're stuck
   - Do NOT try random solutions

2. SEARCH
   - WebSearch: "[error] + [tech] + [CURRENT YEAR]"
   - WebFetch: Official documentation
   - Include current year, not training year

3. UNDERSTAND
   - Read results carefully
   - Identify ROOT CAUSE
   - Know WHY before fixing

4. APPLY
   - Implement correct solution
   - From documentation, not guessing

5. DOCUMENT
   - Update relevant docs if this is a recurring issue
```

---

## Project Structure

```
system/
├── apps/
│   └── canvas/           # Production app (Vite + React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS)
│       ├── src/
│       │   ├── main.tsx          # Entry point
│       │   ├── App.tsx           # Main component
│       │   ├── components/       # BlueprintJS UI components
│       │   ├── canvas/           # R3F rendering
│       │   │   ├── InfiniteCanvas.tsx
│       │   │   ├── CameraController.tsx
│       │   │   └── layers/       # Macro, Meso, Micro
│       │   ├── data/             # Types and generator
│       │   └── hooks/            # useViewport, etc.
│       ├── index.html
│       ├── vite.config.ts
│       └── package.json
├── packages/
│   └── toolkit/          # State machine, utilities (React 18 || 19)
├── design/
│   └── reference/        # POC and visual references
│       └── semantic-zoom-poc.html
├── docs/                 # Domain documentation
├── CLAUDE.md             # This file (project rules)
├── claude-progress.txt   # Session history
├── features.json         # Feature requirements (source of truth)
└── agent_docs/           # Architecture, testing, conventions
```

## Key Files

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `apps/canvas/src/main.tsx` | Production app entry |
| `apps/canvas/src/App.tsx` | Main React component |
| `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` | R3F canvas |
| `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/*.tsx` | LOD rendering layers |
| `apps/canvas/src/data/generator.ts` | Mock data generation |
| `packages/toolkit/` | Shared state machine |
| `URGENT_POC/canvas.html` | Archived POC (reference) |

## LOD System (Level of Detail)

The system implements three rendering layers based on camera zoom:

- **Macro (zoom < 0.5):** Procedural shader heatmap - 1 draw call
- **Meso (0.5 ≤ zoom ≤ 2.0):** InstancedMesh with 60k instances
- **Micro (zoom > 2.0):** HTML overlay with Line2 dependency curves

## Current State

Read `claude-progress.txt` for session history and `features.json` for requirements status.

## Documentation

### Domain Documentation (`docs/`)
- @docs/README.md - Documentation index
- @docs/DOMAIN.md - Fleet, maintenance types, entities
- @docs/REQUIREMENTS.md - Business requirements (from RFQ)
- @docs/UX_SPECIFICATION.md - UX design and interactions
- @docs/RESEARCH.md - Industry research and best practices

### Technical Documentation (`agent_docs/`)
- @agent_docs/architecture.md - Architecture details
- @agent_docs/testing.md - Testing protocol
- @agent_docs/conventions.md - Code conventions
- @agent_docs/ai-limitations.md - AI limitations and mitigations

{

    },
    {

    },
    {

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# MMS System Documentation

This directory contains domain knowledge, requirements, research, and UX specifications for the Metro Integrated Planning System.

## Document Index

| Document | Description | Audience |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| [DOMAIN.md](./DOMAIN.md) | Fleet composition, maintenance types, domain entities | Developers, Domain Experts |
| [REQUIREMENTS.md](./REQUIREMENTS.md) | Business requirements (from RFQ) | Product, Engineering |
| [UX_SPECIFICATION.md](./UX_SPECIFICATION.md) | UX design and interaction patterns | Designers, Frontend Devs |
| [RESEARCH.md](./RESEARCH.md) | Industry research on maintenance scheduling | All stakeholders |

## Project Context

### What is MMS?

The **Metro Integrated Planning System (MMS)** is a high-performance visualization tool for railway rolling stock maintenance planning. It provides a "Single Pane of Glass" solution that unifies:

- **Strategic Planning** (1-12 years): Budget allocation, major overhauls
- **Tactical Planning** (weeks-months): Workload balancing, resource leveling
- **Operational Planning** (daily): Run assignments, track allocation

### The "Semantic Zoom" Concept

Inspired by Google Earth, the system implements **semantic zoom** - the visualization automatically transforms based on zoom level:

| Zoom Level | Threshold | Time Scale | Visualization |
|------------|-----------|------------|---------------|
| Macro | < 0.5x | 10-20 years | Heatmap (yellow→orange→red) |
| Meso | 0.5-2x | 2-5 years | Task blocks (DNA pattern) |
| Micro | > 2x | 4-8 weeks | Detailed cards with labels |

### Current Implementation

**Production:** `apps/canvas` - React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS 6 app with semantic zoom.

```bash
# Run production app (http://localhost:5173)
cd apps/canvas && bun run dev
```

**Production Features (F101-F117):**

| Phase | Features | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| **Foundation** | F101-F103 | Vite + React 18 + BlueprintJS + R3F setup |
| **Core Rendering** | F104-F107 | Data, MesoLayer, pan/zoom, LOD state |
| **All Layers** | F108-F110 | MacroLayer, MicroLayer, smooth transitions |
| **UI Integration** | F111-F114 | Sidebar, timeline, controls, conflicts |
| **Polish** | F115-F117 | Instructions, performance, visual parity |

**POC Validation (Archived):**

The POC (`URGENT_POC/canvas.html`) validated the semantic zoom concept with F001-F015:
- ✅ Pan/zoom navigation, LOD transitions
- ✅ Macro/Meso/Micro layers
- ✅ Sidebar and timeline sync
- ✅ Conflict indicators

See `features.json` for complete feature list and status.

## Relationship to Implementation

### Production App (`apps/canvas`)

The UX patterns in [UX_SPECIFICATION.md](./UX_SPECIFICATION.md) are implemented in:
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/` - R3F rendering components
- `apps/canvas/src/components/` - BlueprintJS UI components
- `apps/canvas/src/hooks/` - State management (useViewport)

### Domain Entities

The domain entities in [DOMAIN.md](./DOMAIN.md) are implemented in:
- `apps/canvas/src/data/types.ts` - TypeScript interfaces
- `apps/canvas/src/data/generator.ts` - Mock data generation

### Archived POC

The validated prototype (`URGENT_POC/canvas.html`) serves as reference:
- Demonstrates semantic zoom concept
- Visual reference for production implementation

### Future Development

The full RFQ requirements in [REQUIREMENTS.md](./REQUIREMENTS.md) include features not yet implemented:
- Real-time mileage data integration
- What-If scenario simulation
- Cost forecasting and budget tracking
- IBM Maximo integration

These represent the product roadmap beyond the current demo.

## Quick Links

- **Technical Architecture**: See `agent_docs/architecture.md`
- **Visual References**: See `design/README.md`
- **Code Conventions**: See `agent_docs/conventions.md`
- **Session Progress**: See `claude-progress.txt`

# MMS System Documentation

This directory contains domain knowledge, requirements, research, and UX specifications for the Metro Integrated Planning System.

## Document Index

| Document | Description | Audience |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| [DOMAIN.md](./DOMAIN.md) | Fleet composition, maintenance types, domain entities | Developers, Domain Experts |
| [REQUIREMENTS.md](./REQUIREMENTS.md) | Business requirements (from RFQ) | Product, Engineering |
| [UX_SPECIFICATION.md](./UX_SPECIFICATION.md) | UX design and interaction patterns | Designers, Frontend Devs |
| [RESEARCH.md](./RESEARCH.md) | Industry research on maintenance scheduling | All stakeholders |

## Project Context

### What is MMS?

The **Metro Integrated Planning System (MMS)** is a high-performance visualization tool for railway rolling stock maintenance planning. It provides a "Single Pane of Glass" solution that unifies:

- **Strategic Planning** (1-12 years): Budget allocation, major overhauls
- **Tactical Planning** (weeks-months): Workload balancing, resource leveling
- **Operational Planning** (daily): Run assignments, track allocation

### The "Semantic Zoom" Concept

Inspired by Google Earth, the system implements **semantic zoom** - the visualization automatically transforms based on zoom level:

| Zoom Level | Threshold | Time Scale | Visualization |
|------------|-----------|------------|---------------|
| Macro | < 0.5x | 10-20 years | Heatmap (yellow→orange→red) |
| Meso | 0.5-2x | 2-5 years | Task blocks (DNA pattern) |
| Micro | > 2x | 4-8 weeks | Detailed cards with labels |

### Current Implementation

**Production:** `apps/canvas` - React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS 6 app with semantic zoom.

```bash
# Run production app (http://localhost:5173)
cd apps/canvas && bun run dev
```

**Production Features (F101-F117):**

| Phase | Features | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| **Foundation** | F101-F103 | Vite + React 18 + BlueprintJS + R3F setup |
| **Core Rendering** | F104-F107 | Data, MesoLayer, pan/zoom, LOD state |
| **All Layers** | F108-F110 | MacroLayer, MicroLayer, smooth transitions |
| **UI Integration** | F111-F114 | Sidebar, timeline, controls, conflicts |
| **Polish** | F115-F117 | Instructions, performance, visual parity |

**POC Reference:**

The POC (`design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html`) serves as visual and algorithmic reference:
- Pan/zoom navigation, LOD transitions
- Macro/Meso/Micro layers
- Sidebar and timeline sync
- Conflict indicators

See `features.json` for complete feature list and status.

## Relationship to Implementation

### Production App (`apps/canvas`)

The UX patterns in [UX_SPECIFICATION.md](./UX_SPECIFICATION.md) are implemented in:
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/` - R3F rendering components
- `apps/canvas/src/components/` - BlueprintJS UI components
- `apps/canvas/src/hooks/` - State management (useViewport)

### Domain Entities

The domain entities in [DOMAIN.md](./DOMAIN.md) are implemented in:
- `apps/canvas/src/data/types.ts` - TypeScript interfaces
- `apps/canvas/src/data/generator.ts` - Mock data generation

### Archived POC

The validated prototype (`URGENT_POC/canvas.html`) serves as reference:
- Demonstrates semantic zoom concept
- Visual reference for production implementation

### Future Development

The full RFQ requirements in [REQUIREMENTS.md](./REQUIREMENTS.md) include features not yet implemented:
- Real-time mileage data integration
- What-If scenario simulation
- Cost forecasting and budget tracking
- IBM Maximo integration

These represent the product roadmap beyond the current demo.

## Quick Links

- **Technical Architecture**: See `agent_docs/architecture.md`
- **Visual References**: See `design/README.md`
- **Code Conventions**: See `agent_docs/conventions.md`
- **Session Progress**: See `claude-progress.txt`

lines: +- Algorithm reference for LOD, heatmap, rendering logic

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# MMS System Architecture

## Overview

The Metro Integrated Planning System is a high-performance WebGL application for metro fleet planning. It implements a "Semantic Zoom" UX similar to Google Earth, switching between three visualization layers based on camera zoom level.

## Technology Choices

### Why Vite?
- Fast HMR (Hot Module Replacement)
- Modern ESM-first architecture
- Excellent TypeScript support
- Simple configuration

### Why React 18?
- Required for BlueprintJS 6.x compatibility
- Concurrent features for smooth UI
- Improved hooks performance
- Better Suspense support

### Why BlueprintJS?
- Enterprise-grade UI components from Palantir
- Consistent design system (Intent colors, Icons)
- Accessible out of the box
- Dark theme support

### Why React Three Fiber?
- Declarative Three.js API
- React component model for 3D scenes
- Excellent ecosystem (drei, three-stdlib)

**Version Pairing (Critical):**
- React 18 → `@react-three/fiber@8.x` + `@react-three/drei@9.x`
- React 19 → `@react-three/fiber@9.x` + `@react-three/drei@10.x`

Since BlueprintJS 6 requires React 18, we use R3F 8.x.

### Why Bun?
- Faster package management
- Native workspace support
- Faster dev server startup

## Project Structure

```
system/
├── apps/
│   └── canvas/                   # Production app (Vite + React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS)
│       ├── index.html            # HTML entry
│       ├── vite.config.ts        # Vite configuration
│       ├── package.json
│       └── src/
│           ├── main.tsx          # React entry point
│           ├── App.tsx           # Main component with layout
│           ├── components/       # BlueprintJS UI components
│           │   ├── AppLayout.tsx     # Navbar, Sidebar, Canvas container
│           │   ├── TrainSidebar.tsx  # Train IDs (Card)
│           │   ├── ZoomControls.tsx  # ButtonGroup +/-
│           │   └── LODIndicator.tsx  # Tag component
│           ├── canvas/           # R3F rendering
│           │   ├── InfiniteCanvas.tsx
│           │   ├── CameraController.tsx
│           │   └── layers/
│           │       ├── MacroLayer.tsx    # Heatmap (InstancedMesh/Shader)
│           │       ├── MesoLayer.tsx     # Task blocks (InstancedMesh)
│           │       └── MicroLayer.tsx    # Detailed cards (Html)
│           ├── data/
│           │   ├── types.ts          # Train, Task interfaces
│           │   └── generator.ts      # Mock data generation
│           └── hooks/
│               └── useViewport.ts    # zoom, pan, LOD state
│
├── packages/
│   └── toolkit/              # State machine & utilities (React 18 || 19)
│       └── src/
│           └── state-machine/ # Core state management
│
├── URGENT_POC/               # Archived POC (reference only)
│   └── canvas.html           # Validated prototype
│
└── design/                   # Visual reference images
    ├── screen-1.png          # Macro view reference
    ├── screen-2.png          # Meso view reference
    └── ...
```

## Data Flow

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    User Interaction                          │
│         (Mouse wheel, drag, keyboard, sidebar scroll)        │
└─────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                                  │
                                  ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    State Machine                             │
│               (packages/gl-engine/state)                     │
│                                                              │
│  Slices:                                                     │
│  ├── viewport: { zoom, x, y, lod }                          │
│  ├── selection: { hovered, selected }                       │
│  ├── schedule: { trains, tasks }                            │
│  └── conflicts: { conflicts[] }                             │
└─────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                                  │
                    ┌─────────────┼─────────────┐
                    │             │             │
                    ▼             ▼             ▼
             ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐
             │  Macro   │  │   Meso   │  │  Micro   │
             │  Layer   │  │   Layer  │  │  Layer   │
             │ (shader) │  │(instanced│  │  (HTML)  │
             └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘
```

## Key Components

### InfiniteCanvas
- **Purpose**: Orchestrates the three rendering layers
- **Location**: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx`
- **Dependencies**: CameraController, MacroLayer, MesoLayer, MicroLayer

### CameraController
- **Purpose**: Manages orthographic camera with pan/zoom
- **Location**: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/CameraController.tsx`
- **Dependencies**: useViewport hook, R3F hooks (useThree, useFrame)

### MacroLayer (Zoom < 0.5)
- **Purpose**: Renders 20-year strategic view as heatmap
- **Location**: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MacroLayer.tsx`
- **Implementation**:
  - InstancedMesh with colored cells
  - Or: ShaderMaterial with DataTexture
  - Yellow→Orange→Red gradient based on task density
- **Performance**: Minimal draw calls

### MesoLayer (0.5 ≤ Zoom ≤ 2.0)
- **Purpose**: Renders task blocks as instanced mesh
- **Location**: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MesoLayer.tsx`
- **Implementation**:
  - InstancedMesh with BoxGeometry
  - InstancedBufferAttribute for colors/transforms
  - Conflict tasks: Intent.DANGER red (#cd4246)
- **Performance**: Single draw call for all instances

### MicroLayer (Zoom > 2.0)
- **Purpose**: Renders detailed task cards with HTML
- **Location**: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MicroLayer.tsx`
- **Implementation**:
  - drei's Html component with transform
  - BlueprintJS styling (Card, Tag)
  - Accessibility (tabIndex, ARIA)

## Key Interfaces

```typescript
interface Train {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  type: 'metro' | 'regional' | 'freight';
}

interface Task {
  id: string;
  trainId: string;
  startDate: Date;
  endDate: Date;
  type: 'maintenance' | 'overhaul' | 'inspection';
  conflict: boolean;
  dependencies: string[];
}

interface Conflict {
  id: string;
  taskIds: [string, string];
  type: 'overlap' | 'resource' | 'dependency';
  severity: 'low' | 'medium' | 'high';
}
```

## Configuration

### Environment Variables
```bash
# No environment variables required for development
# Production may need:
VITE_API_URL=    # If backend is added
```

### Build Configuration
- `apps/canvas/vite.config.ts` - Vite settings
- `apps/canvas/tsconfig.json` - TypeScript config
- `bunfig.toml` - Bun workspace settings

## Performance Considerations

### GPU Optimization
- MacroLayer: Procedural shader (no geometry overhead)
- MesoLayer: InstancedMesh (single draw call for 60k objects)
- Spatial Hash Grid: O(1) hover detection

### Memory Management
- DataTexture: 4096x50 pixels = ~1MB
- InstancedMesh: 60k instances = ~5MB GPU memory
- Careful dispose() on unmount

### React Optimization
- useMemo for derived data
- useCallback for event handlers
- Stable keys for lists

## Version Compatibility Matrix

| Dependency | Version | Reason |
|------------|---------|--------|
| React | 18.3.1 | Required for BlueprintJS 6 |
| @blueprintjs/core | 6.1.0 | UI components |
| @react-three/fiber | 8.17.x | Pairs with React 18 |
| @react-three/drei | 9.114.x | R3F 8.x helpers |
| three | 0.170.x | WebGL engine |
| vite | 5.4.x | Build tool |

**Warning:** Do NOT upgrade to R3F 9.x or drei 10.x without also upgrading to React 19.

## Security Considerations

- No backend currently (client-only)
- Mock data generation is deterministic
- No user input validation needed (display only)

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# @mms/canvas

Production application for the Metro Integrated Planning System - a high-performance "Semantic Zoom" visualization for railway maintenance planning.

## Quick Start

```bash
# From this directory
bun run dev

# Or from root
cd ../.. && bun run dev --filter=@mms/canvas
```

The app runs at http://localhost:5173

## Tech Stack

| Technology | Version | Purpose |
|------------|---------|---------|
| **Vite** | 5.x | Build tool and dev server |
| **React** | 18.3.1 | UI framework (required for BlueprintJS 6) |
| **BlueprintJS** | 6.1.0 | UI component library (Palantir) |
| **R3F** | 8.x | React Three Fiber for WebGL |
| **drei** | 9.x | R3F helpers and components |
| **Three.js** | 0.170.x | 3D rendering engine |

## Project Structure

```
src/
├── main.tsx              # Entry point
├── App.tsx               # Main component with layout
├── components/           # BlueprintJS UI components
│   ├── AppLayout.tsx         # Navbar, Sidebar, Canvas container
│   ├── TrainSidebar.tsx      # Train IDs (Card)
│   ├── ZoomControls.tsx      # ButtonGroup +/-
│   └── LODIndicator.tsx      # Tag component
├── canvas/               # R3F rendering
│   ├── InfiniteCanvas.tsx
│   ├── CameraController.tsx
│   └── layers/
│       ├── MacroLayer.tsx    # Heatmap (InstancedMesh/Shader)
│       ├── MesoLayer.tsx     # Task blocks (InstancedMesh)
│       └── MicroLayer.tsx    # Detailed cards (Html)
├── data/
│   ├── types.ts              # Train, Task interfaces
│   └── generator.ts          # Mock data generation
└── hooks/
    └── useViewport.ts        # zoom, pan, LOD state
```

## LOD System (Level of Detail)

The canvas implements three rendering layers based on camera zoom:

| LOD | Zoom | Visualization | Technical |
|-----|------|---------------|-----------|
| **Macro** | < 0.5x | Heatmap (yellow→orange→red) | InstancedMesh or Shader |
| **Meso** | 0.5-2.0x | Task blocks (DNA pattern) | InstancedMesh |
| **Micro** | > 2.0x | Detailed task cards | drei Html + CSS |

## Features (see features.json)

- **F101**: Vite + React 18 + BlueprintJS skeleton ✅
- **F102-F103**: R3F Canvas + Camera setup
- **F104-F107**: Data, MesoLayer, pan/zoom, LOD
- **F108-F110**: MacroLayer, MicroLayer, transitions
- **F111-F114**: Sidebar, timeline, controls
- **F115-F117**: Instructions, performance, visual parity

## Commands

```bash
bun run dev        # Start dev server
bun run build      # Production build
bun run preview    # Preview production build
bun run typecheck  # Type check
```

## Visual Reference

See `../../design/` for pixel-perfect target specifications.

## Related

- **POC Reference**: `../../URGENT_POC/canvas.html` - Validated prototype
- **Domain Docs**: `../../docs/DOMAIN.md` - Fleet, maintenance types
- **UX Spec**: `../../docs/UX_SPECIFICATION.md` - Interaction patterns

## A questão central:

O POC (`semantic-zoom-poc.html`, antes `canvas.html`) ainda é ou não é a referência para as próximas tasks?

## Análise:

### O que o POC oferece:
1. **Algoritmos funcionais** - generateData(), renderMacroLayer(), renderMesoLayer(), renderMicroLayer(), handleWheel(), handleMouseDown(), etc.
2. **Lógica de LOD** - thresholds, transições
3. **Visual de referência** - como deve ficar o resultado final
4. **Comportamento de referência** - como pan/zoom deve funcionar

### O que as features F104-F117 precisam:
- F104: Data types e mock generator → **Precisa do algoritmo do POC**
- F105: MesoLayer → **Precisa da lógica do POC como referência**
- F106: Pan/Zoom → **Precisa do handleWheel/handleMouse do POC**
- F107: LOD state → **Precisa dos thresholds do POC**
- F108-F110: Layers → **Precisa da lógica de rendering do POC**
- F111-F114: UI components → **Precisa do visual do POC**
- F117: "Full visual parity with POC" → **POC é literalmente o critério de sucesso**

### Conclusão:

**SIM, o POC ainda É a referência principal para as próximas tasks.**

O problema não é que o POC não seja referência - ele É. O problema é que:

1. O PATH mudou de `URGENT_POC/canvas.html` para `design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html`
2. Algumas notas estavam mal escritas (como "canvas.html is the single product entry point" - isso nunca foi verdade, o entry point é `apps/canvas`)


3. Precisamos ajustar os caminhos e a linguagem para deixar claro o papel do POC

Vou corrigir os pontos críticos:
- Atualizar todos os paths para `design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html`

content.text:
Excelente pergunta. Vamos pensar com calma.

---

## O POC ainda é referência?

**SIM, absolutamente.** O POC continua sendo a referência principal para:

| Feature | O que precisa do POC |
|---------|---------------------|
| F104 | `generateData()` - algoritmo de geração de dados |
| F105 | `renderMesoLayer()` - lógica de rendering |
| F106 | `handleWheel()`, `handleMouseDown()` - lógica de pan/zoom |
| F107 | `LOD_THRESHOLDS` - valores de transição |
| F108-F110 | Lógica de cada layer |
| F117 | **"Full visual parity with POC"** - O POC é literalmente o critério de sucesso |

---

## O problema real

O problema **não é** que o POC deixou de ser referência.

O problema é que **algumas notas estavam mal escritas**:

```json
// F001 - ERRADO:
"notes": "Foundation - canvas.html is the single product entry point"
```

Isso diz que `canvas.html` é o **entry point do produto**. Mas isso nunca foi verdade:
- O **produto** é `apps/canvas` (React app)
- O **POC** (`canvas.html`) é apenas **referência**

---

## O que realmente precisa mudar:

| Tipo | De | Para |
|------|-----|------|
| **Path** | `URGENT_POC/canvas.html` | `design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html` |
| **Linguagem** | "entry point", "port from" | "reference", "based on algorithm from" |

### Exemplo de correção:

**F001 notes (antes):**
> "Foundation - canvas.html is the single product entry point"

**F001 notes (depois):**
> "POC validated - semantic zoom concept. Production app: apps/canvas"

**F104 step (antes):**
> "Port generateData() from canvas.html"

**F104 step (depois):**
> "Implement generateData() based on design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html"

---

## Resumo

O POC **continua sendo a referência** para algoritmos e visual. Só precisamos:
1. Atualizar os **paths** para o novo local
2. Corrigir a **linguagem** para deixar claro que é referência, não produto

Concordas com essa análise?

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# MMS System Architecture

## Overview

The Metro Integrated Planning System is a high-performance WebGL application for metro fleet planning. It implements a "Semantic Zoom" UX similar to Google Earth, switching between three visualization layers based on camera zoom level.

## Technology Choices

### Why Vite?
- Fast HMR (Hot Module Replacement)
- Modern ESM-first architecture
- Excellent TypeScript support
- Simple configuration

### Why React 18?
- Required for BlueprintJS 6.x compatibility
- Concurrent features for smooth UI
- Improved hooks performance
- Better Suspense support

### Why BlueprintJS?
- Enterprise-grade UI components from Palantir
- Consistent design system (Intent colors, Icons)
- Accessible out of the box
- Dark theme support

### Why React Three Fiber?
- Declarative Three.js API
- React component model for 3D scenes
- Excellent ecosystem (drei, three-stdlib)

**Version Pairing (Critical):**
- React 18 → `@react-three/fiber@8.x` + `@react-three/drei@9.x`
- React 19 → `@react-three/fiber@9.x` + `@react-three/drei@10.x`

Since BlueprintJS 6 requires React 18, we use R3F 8.x.

### Why Bun?
- Faster package management
- Native workspace support
- Faster dev server startup

## Project Structure

```
system/
├── apps/
│   └── canvas/                   # Production app (Vite + React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS)
│       ├── index.html            # HTML entry
│       ├── vite.config.ts        # Vite configuration
│       ├── package.json
│       └── src/
│           ├── main.tsx          # React entry point
│           ├── App.tsx           # Main component with layout
│           ├── components/       # BlueprintJS UI components
│           │   ├── AppLayout.tsx     # Navbar, Sidebar, Canvas container
│           │   ├── TrainSidebar.tsx  # Train IDs (Card)
│           │   ├── ZoomControls.tsx  # ButtonGroup +/-
│           │   └── LODIndicator.tsx  # Tag component
│           ├── canvas/           # R3F rendering
│           │   ├── InfiniteCanvas.tsx
│           │   ├── CameraController.tsx
│           │   └── layers/
│           │       ├── MacroLayer.tsx    # Heatmap (InstancedMesh/Shader)
│           │       ├── MesoLayer.tsx     # Task blocks (InstancedMesh)
│           │       └── MicroLayer.tsx    # Detailed cards (Html)
│           ├── data/
│           │   ├── types.ts          # Train, Task interfaces
│           │   └── generator.ts      # Mock data generation
│           └── hooks/
│               └── useViewport.ts    # zoom, pan, LOD state
│
├── packages/
│   └── toolkit/              # State machine & utilities (React 18 || 19)
│       └── src/
│           └── state-machine/ # Core state management
│
└── design/
    ├── reference/
    │   └── semantic-zoom-poc.html  # POC for visual/algo reference
    ├── screen-1.png          # Macro view reference
    ├── screen-2.png          # Meso view reference
    └── ...
```

## Data Flow

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    User Interaction                          │
│         (Mouse wheel, drag, keyboard, sidebar scroll)        │
└─────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                                  │
                                  ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    State Machine                             │
│               (packages/gl-engine/state)                     │
│                                                              │
│  Slices:                                                     │
│  ├── viewport: { zoom, x, y, lod }                          │
│  ├── selection: { hovered, selected }                       │
│  ├── schedule: { trains, tasks }                            │
│  └── conflicts: { conflicts[] }                             │
└─────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                                  │
                    ┌─────────────┼─────────────┐
                    │             │             │
                    ▼             ▼             ▼
             ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐
             │  Macro   │  │   Meso   │  │  Micro   │
             │  Layer   │  │   Layer  │  │  Layer   │
             │ (shader) │  │(instanced│  │  (HTML)  │
             └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘
```

## Key Components

### InfiniteCanvas
- **Purpose**: Orchestrates the three rendering layers
- **Location**: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx`
- **Dependencies**: CameraController, MacroLayer, MesoLayer, MicroLayer

### CameraController
- **Purpose**: Manages orthographic camera with pan/zoom
- **Location**: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/CameraController.tsx`
- **Dependencies**: useViewport hook, R3F hooks (useThree, useFrame)

### MacroLayer (Zoom < 0.5)
- **Purpose**: Renders 20-year strategic view as heatmap
- **Location**: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MacroLayer.tsx`
- **Implementation**:
  - InstancedMesh with colored cells
  - Or: ShaderMaterial with DataTexture
  - Yellow→Orange→Red gradient based on task density
- **Performance**: Minimal draw calls

### MesoLayer (0.5 ≤ Zoom ≤ 2.0)
- **Purpose**: Renders task blocks as instanced mesh
- **Location**: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MesoLayer.tsx`
- **Implementation**:
  - InstancedMesh with BoxGeometry
  - InstancedBufferAttribute for colors/transforms
  - Conflict tasks: Intent.DANGER red (#cd4246)
- **Performance**: Single draw call for all instances

### MicroLayer (Zoom > 2.0)
- **Purpose**: Renders detailed task cards with HTML
- **Location**: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MicroLayer.tsx`
- **Implementation**:
  - drei's Html component with transform
  - BlueprintJS styling (Card, Tag)
  - Accessibility (tabIndex, ARIA)

## Key Interfaces

```typescript
interface Train {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  type: 'metro' | 'regional' | 'freight';
}

interface Task {
  id: string;
  trainId: string;
  startDate: Date;
  endDate: Date;
  type: 'maintenance' | 'overhaul' | 'inspection';
  conflict: boolean;
  dependencies: string[];
}

interface Conflict {
  id: string;
  taskIds: [string, string];
  type: 'overlap' | 'resource' | 'dependency';
  severity: 'low' | 'medium' | 'high';
}
```

## Configuration

### Environment Variables
```bash
# No environment variables required for development
# Production may need:
VITE_API_URL=    # If backend is added
```

### Build Configuration
- `apps/canvas/vite.config.ts` - Vite settings
- `apps/canvas/tsconfig.json` - TypeScript config
- `bunfig.toml` - Bun workspace settings

## Performance Considerations

### GPU Optimization
- MacroLayer: Procedural shader (no geometry overhead)
- MesoLayer: InstancedMesh (single draw call for 60k objects)
- Spatial Hash Grid: O(1) hover detection

### Memory Management
- DataTexture: 4096x50 pixels = ~1MB
- InstancedMesh: 60k instances = ~5MB GPU memory
- Careful dispose() on unmount

### React Optimization
- useMemo for derived data
- useCallback for event handlers
- Stable keys for lists

## Version Compatibility Matrix

| Dependency | Version | Reason |
|------------|---------|--------|
| React | 18.3.1 | Required for BlueprintJS 6 |
| @blueprintjs/core | 6.1.0 | UI components |
| @react-three/fiber | 8.17.x | Pairs with React 18 |
| @react-three/drei | 9.114.x | R3F 8.x helpers |
| three | 0.170.x | WebGL engine |
| vite | 5.4.x | Build tool |

**Warning:** Do NOT upgrade to R3F 9.x or drei 10.x without also upgrading to React 19.

## Security Considerations

- No backend currently (client-only)
- Mock data generation is deterministic
- No user input validation needed (display only)

Analysis:

1. **Initial Context**: The user is working on a project called MMS (Metro Integrated Planning System) that uses a "Harness" framework for AI agents to work across multiple sessions. The user selected line 79 from docs/README.md referencing "URGENT_POC/canvas.html".

2. **User's Initial Request**: The user asked to evaluate whether URGENT_POC/canvas.html is useful for the harness/development. If not, remove docs about it and keep it as a simple example. If useful, move it to docs folder as canvas-example.html and remove historical data about it from harness and docs - it should serve only as a simple example.

3. **My Initial Analysis**: I read the POC file (canvas.html - ~615 lines), the production app (apps/canvas/src/App.tsx), features.json, and analyzed the structure. I concluded the POC has value as visual/algorithmic reference but is not useful for the harness workflow since work is done in apps/canvas.

4. **First Attempt (Too Aggressive)**: I made extensive changes:

7. **User Feedback #3**: "Hmm, desfaca suas alteracoes, comece de novo fazendo apenas o que eu pedi inicialmente - cuidado pois tem alteracoes no projeto que não sao suas e vc nao deve tocar nelas" - Undo changes, start over doing only what was initially asked, be careful as there are changes in the project that aren't mine and shouldn't be touched.

8. **Restoration**: I used git checkout to restore CLAUDE.md, features.json, docs/README.md, and URGENT_POC/. Also removed the design/reference directory I created.

13. **Critical User Feedback #6**: "Espere, precisamos pensar, se o canvas nao for mais a referencia para as proximas tasks, o que seria? Ou ele não é?" - Wait, we need to think - if the canvas is no longer the reference for next tasks, what would be? Or isn't it?

# @mms/canvas

Production application for the Metro Integrated Planning System - a high-performance "Semantic Zoom" visualization for railway maintenance planning.

## Quick Start

```bash
# From this directory
bun run dev

# Or from root
cd ../.. && bun run dev --filter=@mms/canvas
```

The app runs at http://localhost:5173

## Tech Stack

| Technology | Version | Purpose |
|------------|---------|---------|
| **Vite** | 5.x | Build tool and dev server |
| **React** | 18.3.1 | UI framework (required for BlueprintJS 6) |
| **BlueprintJS** | 6.1.0 | UI component library (Palantir) |
| **R3F** | 8.x | React Three Fiber for WebGL |
| **drei** | 9.x | R3F helpers and components |
| **Three.js** | 0.170.x | 3D rendering engine |

## Project Structure

```
src/
├── main.tsx              # Entry point
├── App.tsx               # Main component with layout
├── components/           # BlueprintJS UI components
│   ├── AppLayout.tsx         # Navbar, Sidebar, Canvas container
│   ├── TrainSidebar.tsx      # Train IDs (Card)
│   ├── ZoomControls.tsx      # ButtonGroup +/-
│   └── LODIndicator.tsx      # Tag component
├── canvas/               # R3F rendering
│   ├── InfiniteCanvas.tsx
│   ├── CameraController.tsx
│   └── layers/
│       ├── MacroLayer.tsx    # Heatmap (InstancedMesh/Shader)
│       ├── MesoLayer.tsx     # Task blocks (InstancedMesh)
│       └── MicroLayer.tsx    # Detailed cards (Html)
├── data/
│   ├── types.ts              # Train, Task interfaces
│   └── generator.ts          # Mock data generation
└── hooks/
    └── useViewport.ts        # zoom, pan, LOD state
```

## LOD System (Level of Detail)

The canvas implements three rendering layers based on camera zoom:

| LOD | Zoom | Visualization | Technical |
|-----|------|---------------|-----------|
| **Macro** | < 0.5x | Heatmap (yellow→orange→red) | InstancedMesh or Shader |
| **Meso** | 0.5-2.0x | Task blocks (DNA pattern) | InstancedMesh |
| **Micro** | > 2.0x | Detailed task cards | drei Html + CSS |

## Features (see features.json)

- **F101**: Vite + React 18 + BlueprintJS skeleton ✅
- **F102-F103**: R3F Canvas + Camera setup
- **F104-F107**: Data, MesoLayer, pan/zoom, LOD
- **F108-F110**: MacroLayer, MicroLayer, transitions
- **F111-F114**: Sidebar, timeline, controls
- **F115-F117**: Instructions, performance, visual parity

## Commands

```bash
bun run dev        # Start dev server
bun run build      # Production build
bun run preview    # Preview production build
bun run typecheck  # Type check
```

## Visual Reference

See `../../design/` for pixel-perfect target specifications.

## Related

- **POC Reference**: `../../design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html` - Visual/algorithm reference
- **Domain Docs**: `../../docs/DOMAIN.md` - Fleet, maintenance types
- **UX Spec**: `../../docs/UX_SPECIFICATION.md` - Interaction patterns

1→# Metro Integrated Planning System (Monorepo)
     2→
     3→High-Performance Enterprise Integrated Planning System with "Semantic Zoom" UX (Google Earth-style).
     4→
     5→## Commands
     6→
     7→```bash
     8→bun i              # Install dependencies
     9→bun run dev        # Start development server
    10→bun run build      # Production build
    11→bun run typecheck  # Type check all packages
    12→bun run lint       # Lint all packages
    13→bun run test       # Run tests
    14→```
    15→
    16→## Tech Stack
    17→
    18→- **Runtime:** Bun
    19→- **Monorepo:** Turborepo
    20→- **Build Tool:** Vite 5.x
    21→- **Frontend:** React 18.3.1 (required for BlueprintJS 6)
    22→- **UI Components:** BlueprintJS 6.1.0 (Palantir)
    23→- **Graphics Engine:** @react-three/fiber 8.x, @react-three/drei 9.x, three 0.170.x
    24→- **State Management:** Custom state-machine (packages/toolkit)
    25→
    26→## Project Structure
    27→
    28→```
    29→system/
    30→├── apps/canvas/          # Production React app (Vite + R3F + BlueprintJS)
    31→├── packages/
    32→│   └── toolkit/          # State machine, utilities
    33→├── URGENT_POC/           # Archived POC (reference only)
    34→└── design/               # Visual reference specifications
    35→```
    36→
    37→## Visual References (design/)
    38→
    39→The `design/` directory contains the visual specifications that this system must replicate. See [design/README.md](./design/README.md) for detailed mapping between images and implementation layers.
    40→
    41→| Image | View | Description |
    42→|-------|------|-------------|
    43→| screen-1.png | Macro | Strategic 10-20 year heatmap overview |
    44→| screen-2.png | Meso | Tactical DNA-like allocation strips |
    45→| screen-3.jpg | Micro | Operational task cards with dependencies |
    46→| screen-4.png | Meso | Dense diagonal allocation pattern |
    47→| screen-5.png | Meso | Color-coded resource allocation |
    48→| screen-6.png | Micro | Task details with conflict indicators |
    49→| screen-7.png | UI | Header/sidebar layout reference |
    50→
    51→## LOD System (Level of Detail)
    52→
    53→The system implements three rendering layers based on camera zoom:
    54→
    55→- **Macro (zoom < 0.5):** Procedural shader heatmap - 1 draw call
    56→- **Meso (0.5 ≤ zoom ≤ 2.0):** InstancedMesh with 60k instances
    57→- **Micro (zoom > 2.0):** HTML overlay with Line2 dependency curves
    58→
    59→## Documentation
    60→
    61→- `docs/` - Domain documentation (requirements, UX specs, research)
    62→- `agent_docs/` - Technical documentation for AI agents
    63→- `design/` - Visual reference specifications
    64→
    65→---
    66→
    67→# Harness Protocol for Long-Running AI Agents
    68→
    69→## What is a Harness?
    70→
    71→A **harness** is a framework that enables AI agents to work effectively on complex tasks that span multiple sessions. Created by **Anthropic** as part of the Claude Agent SDK (formerly Claude Code SDK), the harness addresses a fundamental challenge:
    72→
    73→> "The core challenge of long-running agents is that they must work in discrete sessions, and each new session begins with no memory of what came before."
    74→
    75→The harness acts as a **coordination layer** wrapped around ordinary Claude sessions. It tracks files, tasks, and test results across hours of execution, spinning up fresh conversations whenever one thread gets too bloated to stay coherent.
    76→
    77→## Why Anthropic Created the Harness
    78→
    79→After more than six months shipping updates to Claude Code, Anthropic solved hard problems:
    80→- How agents should manage **memory across long-running tasks**
    81→- How to handle **permission systems** that balance autonomy with user control
    82→- How to **coordinate subagents** working toward a shared goal
    83→
    84→The key insight was finding a way for agents to quickly understand the state of work when starting with a fresh context window.
    85→
    86→## Core Architecture
    87→
    88→```
    89→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    90→│                    Harness Framework                         │
    91→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    92→│                                                              │
    93→│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐  │
    94→│  │  Initializer │ → │    Coding    │ → │    Coding    │  │
    95→│  │    Agent     │    │    Agent     │    │    Agent     │  │
    96→│  │  (Session 1) │    │  (Session 2) │    │  (Session N) │  │
    97→│  └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘  │
    98→│         │                   │                   │           │
    99→│         └───────────────────┼───────────────────┘           │
   100→│                             │                               │
   101→│                             ▼                               │
   102→│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
   103→│  │              Shared State Files                       │  │
   104→│  │  • claude-progress.txt  (session history)            │  │
   105→│  │  • features.json        (source of truth)            │  │
   106→│  │  • CLAUDE.md            (project context)            │  │
   107→│  │  • git history          (code changes)               │  │
   108→│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
   109→│                                                              │
   110→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   111→```
   112→
   113→## Two-Agent Architecture
   114→
   115→Anthropic recommends a **two-fold approach**:
   116→
   117→### 1. Initializer Agent (First Session)
   118→- Sets up the initial environment
   119→- Creates the harness files (features.json, claude-progress.txt)
   120→- Establishes project structure
   121→- Runs on a specialized prompt optimized for setup
   122→
   123→### 2. Coding Agent (Subsequent Sessions)
   124→- Makes incremental progress on features
   125→- Leaves artifacts for the next session
   126→- Maintains clean code suitable for merging
   127→- Commits progress to git with descriptive messages
   128→
   129→## Core Files
   130→
   131→| File | Purpose |
   132→|------|---------|
   133→| `CLAUDE.md` | Project context, instructions, and conventions |
   134→| `claude-progress.txt` | Session history and current state |
   135→| `features.json` | Feature requirements with pass/fail status (source of truth) |
   136→| `agent_docs/` | Additional documentation for the agent |
   137→| `init.sh` | Script to start dev server and run basic tests |
   138→| `CLAUDE_NOTES.md` | Learnings and gotchas for future sessions |
   139→
   140→## The Work Loop
   141→
   142→When a session starts, the agent follows this loop:
   143→
   144→```
   145→1. READ claude-progress.txt
   146→   → Understand current state and what was done before
   147→
   148→2. READ features.json
   149→   → Identify next feature to implement (passes: false)
   150→
   151→3. RUN init.sh
   152→   → Start dev server, verify app is in working state
   153→
   154→4. IMPLEMENT one feature at a time
   155→   → Focus on single feature, don't try to do everything
   156→
   157→5. TEST the feature
   158→   → Verify it works as specified in steps
   159→
   160→6. COMMIT changes
   161→   → Granular commits with descriptive messages
   162→
   163→7. UPDATE features.json
   164→   → Mark feature as passes: true
   165→
   166→8. UPDATE claude-progress.txt
   167→   → Log what was done for next session
   168→
   169→9. REPEAT or STOP
   170→   → Continue to next feature or end session
   171→```
   172→
   173→## Critical AI Limitations & Mitigations
   174→
   175→> **IMPORTANT**: AI agents have fundamental limitations that this harness addresses.
   176→
   177→### The Blind Spot Problem
   178→
   179→**AI cannot see what it creates.** When building UI/apps, the AI:
   180→- Cannot see the rendered output
   181→- Often assumes blank screens are "working" because code compiled
   182→- Marks features as complete based on code logic, not visual verification
   183→- May claim success when the app shows nothing or errors
   184→
   185→**Solution**: Mandatory visual testing with MCP after every feature.
   186→
   187→### The Stale Knowledge Problem
   188→
   189→**AI training data is outdated.** Claude's knowledge cutoff means:
   190→- Library APIs may have changed
   191→- Configuration patterns may be deprecated
   192→- New versions have different syntax
   193→- Best practices may have evolved
   194→
   195→**Solution**: Always check the current date and search for updated documentation.
   196→
   197→### The Guessing Problem
   198→
   199→**AI tends to guess instead of research.** When stuck, AI will:
   200→- Try random solutions repeatedly
   201→- Waste context on failed attempts
   202→- Not realize when it's going in circles
   203→
   204→**Solution**: Mandatory research protocol for non-obvious errors.
   205→
   206→---
   207→
   208→## Best Practices
   209→
   210→### 1. One Feature at a Time
   211→Don't attempt to complete everything at once. Focus on a single feature, test it, commit it, then move to the next.
   212→
   213→### 2. Clean State Between Sessions
   214→Leave the environment in a clean state:
   215→- No major bugs
   216→- Well-documented code
   217→- Orderly structure
   218→- Tests passing
   219→
   220→### 3. features.json is Source of Truth
   221→Never declare the project complete without all features passing. The `passes` field should only change from `false` to `true` after verification.
   222→
   223→### 4. Commit After Each Feature
   224→Maintain granular git history. Each feature should have its own commit(s).
   225→
   226→### 5. Research Before Guessing
   227→When encountering errors, classify them:
   228→- **Simple errors** (typos, syntax): Fix directly
   229→- **Complex errors** (runtime, config): Research online first
   230→
   231→### 6. Test Before Marking Complete
   232→Always verify the feature works before marking `passes: true`.
   233→
   234→### 7. ALWAYS Check Current Date
   235→Before starting work, verify the current date. **Do NOT assume you are in the year of your training data.** Use the system date to ensure you search for current documentation.
   236→
   237→### 8. Install Dependencies via Commands
   238→**NEVER manually edit package.json/Cargo.toml/requirements.txt to add dependencies.**
   239→
   240→```bash
   241→# CORRECT - Let package manager resolve versions
   242→bun add react
   243→npm install express
   244→pip install requests
   245→cargo add serde
   246→
   247→# WRONG - Manual version specification
   248→# Editing package.json: "react": "^18.0.0"  <- DON'T DO THIS
   249→```
   250→
   251→Why: Package managers install the latest stable compatible versions and handle peer dependencies correctly.
   252→
   253→---
   254→
   255→## MCP Visual Testing (MANDATORY)
   256→
   257→### Why Visual Testing is Required
   258→
   259→AI agents **cannot see rendered UI**. This creates a critical gap:
   260→
   261→```
   262→What AI knows:          What actually happens:
   263→✓ Code compiled         ✗ Blank white screen
   264→✓ No TypeScript errors  ✗ React error boundary
   265→✓ Tests passed          ✗ Component doesn't render
   266→✓ Server running        ✗ CSS completely broken
   267→```
   268→
   269→**Without visual verification, AI will mark broken features as complete.**
   270→
   271→### Visual Testing Protocol
   272→
   273→**See CLAUDE.md section "MCP VISUAL TESTING (MANDATORY)"** for the complete protocol.
   274→
   275→Summary: Use **Playwright MCP** (headless, does not steal focus):
   276→
   277→```
   278→1. mcp__playwright__browser_navigate url="..."
   279→2. mcp__playwright__browser_console_messages level="error"
   280→3. mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot filename="..."
   281→4. Analyze screenshot
   282→5. mcp__playwright__browser_close
   283→```
   284→
   285→---
   286→
   287→## Error Research Protocol
   288→
   289→### When to Research vs. Fix Directly
   290→
   291→| Error Type | Example | Action |
   292→|------------|---------|--------|
   293→| **Obvious** | Typo in variable name | Fix directly |
   294→| **Syntax** | Missing semicolon | Fix directly |
   295→| **Import** | Wrong import path | Fix directly |
   296→| **Config** | Webpack/Vite config | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
   297→| **Runtime** | Module not found | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
   298→| **Library** | API changed | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
   299→| **Unknown** | Cryptic error | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
   300→
   301→### The STOP-SEARCH-UNDERSTAND-APPLY Method
   302→
   303→When encountering non-obvious errors:
   304→
   305→```
   306→1. STOP
   307→   - Do NOT try random solutions
   308→   - Do NOT keep making changes hoping something works
   309→   - Recognize when you're stuck
   310→
   311→2. SEARCH
   312→   - WebSearch: "[error message] + [technology] + [current year]"
   313→   - WebFetch: Read official documentation
   314→   - Include the CURRENT YEAR in searches (not your training year)
   315→
   316→3. UNDERSTAND
   317→   - Read the search results carefully
   318→   - Identify the ROOT CAUSE
   319→   - Understand WHY the error occurs
   320→
   321→4. APPLY
   322→   - Implement the CORRECT solution
   323→   - Not a workaround or hack
   324→   - The solution from documentation
   325→
   326→5. DOCUMENT
   327→   - Add to CLAUDE_NOTES.md if it's a recurring issue
   328→   - Note in claude-progress.txt
   329→```
   330→
   331→### Example: Research Flow
   332→
   333→```
   334→Error: "Module '@tauri-apps/api/fs' not found"
   335→
   336→❌ WRONG approach:
   337→   → Try different import paths randomly
   338→   → Downgrade versions blindly
   339→   → Copy code from training data (outdated)
   340→
   341→✅ CORRECT approach:
   342→   1. STOP - This is a config/library error
   343→   2. SEARCH - "tauri 2.0 fs module not found 2025"
   344→   3. UNDERSTAND - Tauri 2.0 moved fs to plugin system
   345→   4. APPLY - Install @tauri-apps/plugin-fs, update imports
   346→   5. DOCUMENT - Note in CLAUDE_NOTES.md
   347→```
   348→
   349→---
   350→
   351→## Session Startup Sequence
   352→
   353→Every coding agent session should begin with:
   354→
   355→```bash
   356→# 1. Confirm working directory
   357→pwd
   358→
   359→# 2. Read progress to understand state
   360→cat claude-progress.txt
   361→
   362→# 3. Check recent git history
   363→git log --oneline -10
   364→
   365→# 4. Read feature requirements
   366→cat features.json
   367→
   368→# 5. Start development server
   369→./init.sh
   370→
   371→# 6. Verify app is working
   372→# (run basic tests or visual check)
   373→
   374→# 7. Begin work on next feature
   375→```
   376→
   377→## Context Management
   378→
   379→The Claude Agent SDK implements automatic **compaction** when context limit approaches. However, you can help by:
   380→
   381→- Using `/clear` between unrelated tasks
   382→- Delegating research to subagents
   383→- Keeping CLAUDE.md concise
   384→- Writing clear, focused commit messages
   385→
   386→## Results
   387→
   388→Using this harness approach, Anthropic has observed:
   389→- Claude maintaining focus for **30+ hours** on complex tasks
   390→- Agents completing multi-week projects across sessions
   391→- Consistent quality without context degradation
   392→
   393→## Official Sources
   394→
   395→- [Effective harnesses for long-running agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents)
   396→- [Building agents with the Claude Agent SDK](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-agents-with-the-claude-agent-sdk)
   397→- [Claude Code: Best practices for agentic coding](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices)
   398→
