# Metro Integrated Planning System (Monorepo)

High-Performance Enterprise Integrated Planning System with "Semantic Zoom" UX (Google Earth-style).

## Commands

```bash
bun i              # Install dependencies
bun run dev        # Start development server
bun run build      # Production build
bun run typecheck  # Type check all packages
bun run lint       # Lint all packages
bun run test       # Run tests
```

## Tech Stack

- **Runtime:** Bun
- **Monorepo:** Turborepo
- **Build Tool:** Vite 5.x
- **Frontend:** React 18.3.1 (required for BlueprintJS 6)
- **UI Components:** BlueprintJS 6.1.0 (Palantir)
- **Graphics Engine:** @react-three/fiber 8.x, @react-three/drei 9.x, three 0.170.x
- **State Management:** Custom state-machine (packages/toolkit)

## Project Structure

```
system/
├── apps/canvas/          # Production React app (Vite + R3F + BlueprintJS)
├── packages/
│   └── toolkit/          # State machine, utilities
├── URGENT_POC/           # Archived POC (reference only)
└── design/               # Visual reference specifications
```

## Visual References (design/)

The `design/` directory contains the visual specifications that this system must replicate. See [design/README.md](./design/README.md) for detailed mapping between images and implementation layers.

| Image | View | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| screen-1.png | Macro | Strategic 10-20 year heatmap overview |
| screen-2.png | Meso | Tactical DNA-like allocation strips |
| screen-3.jpg | Micro | Operational task cards with dependencies |
| screen-4.png | Meso | Dense diagonal allocation pattern |
| screen-5.png | Meso | Color-coded resource allocation |
| screen-6.png | Micro | Task details with conflict indicators |
| screen-7.png | UI | Header/sidebar layout reference |

## LOD System (Level of Detail)

The system implements three rendering layers based on camera zoom:

- **Macro (zoom < 0.5):** Procedural shader heatmap - 1 draw call
- **Meso (0.5 ≤ zoom ≤ 2.0):** InstancedMesh with 60k instances
- **Micro (zoom > 2.0):** HTML overlay with Line2 dependency curves

## Documentation

- `docs/` - Domain documentation (requirements, UX specs, research)
- `agent_docs/` - Technical documentation for AI agents
- `design/` - Visual reference specifications

---

# Harness Protocol for Long-Running AI Agents

## What is a Harness?

A **harness** is a framework that enables AI agents to work effectively on complex tasks that span multiple sessions. Created by **Anthropic** as part of the Claude Agent SDK (formerly Claude Code SDK), the harness addresses a fundamental challenge:

> "The core challenge of long-running agents is that they must work in discrete sessions, and each new session begins with no memory of what came before."

The harness acts as a **coordination layer** wrapped around ordinary Claude sessions. It tracks files, tasks, and test results across hours of execution, spinning up fresh conversations whenever one thread gets too bloated to stay coherent.

## Why Anthropic Created the Harness

After more than six months shipping updates to Claude Code, Anthropic solved hard problems:
- How agents should manage **memory across long-running tasks**
- How to handle **permission systems** that balance autonomy with user control
- How to **coordinate subagents** working toward a shared goal

The key insight was finding a way for agents to quickly understand the state of work when starting with a fresh context window.

## Core Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Harness Framework                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐  │
│  │  Initializer │ → │    Coding    │ → │    Coding    │  │
│  │    Agent     │    │    Agent     │    │    Agent     │  │
│  │  (Session 1) │    │  (Session 2) │    │  (Session N) │  │
│  └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘  │
│         │                   │                   │           │
│         └───────────────────┼───────────────────┘           │
│                             │                               │
│                             ▼                               │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │              Shared State Files                       │  │
│  │  • claude-progress.txt  (session history)            │  │
│  │  • features.json        (source of truth)            │  │
│  │  • CLAUDE.md            (project context)            │  │
│  │  • git history          (code changes)               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Two-Agent Architecture

Anthropic recommends a **two-fold approach**:

### 1. Initializer Agent (First Session)
- Sets up the initial environment
- Creates the harness files (features.json, claude-progress.txt)
- Establishes project structure
- Runs on a specialized prompt optimized for setup

### 2. Coding Agent (Subsequent Sessions)
- Makes incremental progress on features
- Leaves artifacts for the next session
- Maintains clean code suitable for merging
- Commits progress to git with descriptive messages

## Core Files

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `CLAUDE.md` | Project context, instructions, and conventions |
| `claude-progress.txt` | Session history and current state |
| `features.json` | Feature requirements with pass/fail status (source of truth) |
| `agent_docs/` | Additional documentation for the agent |
| `init.sh` | Script to start dev server and run basic tests |
| `CLAUDE_NOTES.md` | Learnings and gotchas for future sessions |

## The Work Loop

When a session starts, the agent follows this loop:

```
1. READ claude-progress.txt
   → Understand current state and what was done before

2. READ features.json
   → Identify next feature to implement (passes: false)

3. RUN init.sh
   → Start dev server, verify app is in working state

4. IMPLEMENT one feature at a time
   → Focus on single feature, don't try to do everything

5. TEST the feature
   → Verify it works as specified in steps

6. COMMIT changes
   → Granular commits with descriptive messages

7. UPDATE features.json
   → Mark feature as passes: true

8. UPDATE claude-progress.txt
   → Log what was done for next session

9. REPEAT or STOP
   → Continue to next feature or end session
```

## Critical AI Limitations & Mitigations

> **IMPORTANT**: AI agents have fundamental limitations that this harness addresses.

### The Blind Spot Problem

**AI cannot see what it creates.** When building UI/apps, the AI:
- Cannot see the rendered output
- Often assumes blank screens are "working" because code compiled
- Marks features as complete based on code logic, not visual verification
- May claim success when the app shows nothing or errors

**Solution**: Mandatory visual testing with MCP after every feature.

### The Stale Knowledge Problem

**AI training data is outdated.** Claude's knowledge cutoff means:
- Library APIs may have changed
- Configuration patterns may be deprecated
- New versions have different syntax
- Best practices may have evolved

**Solution**: Always check the current date and search for updated documentation.

### The Guessing Problem

**AI tends to guess instead of research.** When stuck, AI will:
- Try random solutions repeatedly
- Waste context on failed attempts
- Not realize when it's going in circles

**Solution**: Mandatory research protocol for non-obvious errors.

---

## Best Practices

### 1. One Feature at a Time
Don't attempt to complete everything at once. Focus on a single feature, test it, commit it, then move to the next.

### 2. Clean State Between Sessions
Leave the environment in a clean state:
- No major bugs
- Well-documented code
- Orderly structure
- Tests passing

### 3. features.json is Source of Truth
Never declare the project complete without all features passing. The `passes` field should only change from `false` to `true` after verification.

### 4. Commit After Each Feature
Maintain granular git history. Each feature should have its own commit(s).

### 5. Research Before Guessing
When encountering errors, classify them:
- **Simple errors** (typos, syntax): Fix directly
- **Complex errors** (runtime, config): Research online first

### 6. Test Before Marking Complete
Always verify the feature works before marking `passes: true`.

### 7. ALWAYS Check Current Date
Before starting work, verify the current date. **Do NOT assume you are in the year of your training data.** Use the system date to ensure you search for current documentation.

### 8. Install Dependencies via Commands
**NEVER manually edit package.json/Cargo.toml/requirements.txt to add dependencies.**

```bash
# CORRECT - Let package manager resolve versions
bun add react
npm install express
pip install requests
cargo add serde

# WRONG - Manual version specification
# Editing package.json: "react": "^18.0.0"  <- DON'T DO THIS
```

Why: Package managers install the latest stable compatible versions and handle peer dependencies correctly.

---

## MCP Visual Testing (MANDATORY)

### Why Visual Testing is Required

AI agents **cannot see rendered UI**. This creates a critical gap:

```
What AI knows:          What actually happens:
✓ Code compiled         ✗ Blank white screen
✓ No TypeScript errors  ✗ React error boundary
✓ Tests passed          ✗ Component doesn't render
✓ Server running        ✗ CSS completely broken
```

**Without visual verification, AI will mark broken features as complete.**

### Visual Testing Protocol

**See CLAUDE.md section "MCP VISUAL TESTING (MANDATORY)"** for the complete protocol.

Summary: Use **Playwright MCP** (headless, does not steal focus):

```
1. mcp__playwright__browser_navigate url="..."
2. mcp__playwright__browser_console_messages level="error"
3. mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot filename="..."
4. Analyze screenshot
5. mcp__playwright__browser_close
```

---

## Error Research Protocol

### When to Research vs. Fix Directly

| Error Type | Example | Action |
|------------|---------|--------|
| **Obvious** | Typo in variable name | Fix directly |
| **Syntax** | Missing semicolon | Fix directly |
| **Import** | Wrong import path | Fix directly |
| **Config** | Webpack/Vite config | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
| **Runtime** | Module not found | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
| **Library** | API changed | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
| **Unknown** | Cryptic error | **RESEARCH FIRST** |

### The STOP-SEARCH-UNDERSTAND-APPLY Method

When encountering non-obvious errors:

```
1. STOP
   - Do NOT try random solutions
   - Do NOT keep making changes hoping something works
   - Recognize when you're stuck

2. SEARCH
   - WebSearch: "[error message] + [technology] + [current year]"
   - WebFetch: Read official documentation
   - Include the CURRENT YEAR in searches (not your training year)

3. UNDERSTAND
   - Read the search results carefully
   - Identify the ROOT CAUSE
   - Understand WHY the error occurs

4. APPLY
   - Implement the CORRECT solution
   - Not a workaround or hack
   - The solution from documentation

5. DOCUMENT
   - Add to CLAUDE_NOTES.md if it's a recurring issue
   - Note in claude-progress.txt
```

### Example: Research Flow

```
Error: "Module '@tauri-apps/api/fs' not found"

❌ WRONG approach:
   → Try different import paths randomly
   → Downgrade versions blindly
   → Copy code from training data (outdated)

✅ CORRECT approach:
   1. STOP - This is a config/library error
   2. SEARCH - "tauri 2.0 fs module not found 2025"
   3. UNDERSTAND - Tauri 2.0 moved fs to plugin system
   4. APPLY - Install @tauri-apps/plugin-fs, update imports
   5. DOCUMENT - Note in CLAUDE_NOTES.md
```

---

## Session Startup Sequence

Every coding agent session should begin with:

```bash
# 1. Confirm working directory
pwd

# 2. Read progress to understand state
cat claude-progress.txt

# 3. Check recent git history
git log --oneline -10

# 4. Read feature requirements
cat features.json

# 5. Start development server
./init.sh

# 6. Verify app is working
# (run basic tests or visual check)

# 7. Begin work on next feature
```

## Context Management

The Claude Agent SDK implements automatic **compaction** when context limit approaches. However, you can help by:

- Using `/clear` between unrelated tasks
- Delegating research to subagents
- Keeping CLAUDE.md concise
- Writing clear, focused commit messages

## Results

Using this harness approach, Anthropic has observed:
- Claude maintaining focus for **30+ hours** on complex tasks
- Agents completing multi-week projects across sessions
- Consistent quality without context degradation

## Official Sources

- [Effective harnesses for long-running agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents)
- [Building agents with the Claude Agent SDK](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-agents-with-the-claude-agent-sdk)
- [Claude Code: Best practices for agentic coding](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices)

# Visual Design Specifications

This directory contains the **Visual Reference Images** for the Metro Integrated Planning System.

> **Important:** These images are AI-generated and represent the VISION, not pixel-perfect specifications.
> They capture the essence of what we're building: a "Single Pane of Glass" with semantic zoom.

---

## Core Design Vision

### The "80% Cognitive Load Reduction" Goal

The system aims to reduce analysis and planning overhead by 80%+ through:

| Principle                   | Description                                | Implementation                  |
|-----------------------------|--------------------------------------------|---------------------------------|
| **Single Pane of Glass**    | Everything in one view, no page navigation | Pan/zoom instead of tabs        |
| **Semantic Zoom**           | Context changes with zoom level            | 3 LOD layers (Macro/Meso/Micro) |
| **Direct Manipulation**     | Drag to reschedule, not forms              | Future: drag-and-drop tasks     |
| **Game-like Visualization** | WebGL beyond standard charts               | R3F with custom shaders         |

### Design Inspirations

- **Google Earth/Maps:** Seamless zoom from planet to street
- **Strategy Games:** Information density that doesn't overwhelm (Factorio, Cities Skylines)
- **Financial Terminals:** Dense data, clear hierarchy (Bloomberg Terminal)

---

## Screen Reference Analysis

### screen-1.png: Simulation Setup

![Simulation Setup](screen-1.png)

**What it shows:**
- Maintenance Heatmap in background
- Modal: "Simulation Setup: Scenario A"
- Constraint sliders (Labor Availability: 50%)
- Date range selector
- Infrastructure toggle (Maintenance Pit Status)
- "Run Solver (Est. 45s)" button

**Design Intent:**
- What-If scenarios start with constraint adjustment
- Solver is async with time estimate
- Simple controls for complex simulations

**Implementation:** Future feature (Scenario Mode)

---

### screen-2.png: Full System with Zoom Popup

![Full System](screen-2.png)

**What it shows:**
- Header KPIs: "Fleet Avail: 98%", "Annual Budget: On Track"
- Maintenance Heatmap (2021-2022+)
- Legend: Routine maintenance, Mid-life overhauls, Capacity bottlenecks
- Zoom popup: "Zoom in 4 weeks" detail view
- Tasks with durations: "Brake Rev. T104 (8h)", "Wheel Lathe T112 (4h)"
- Dependency arrows between tasks
- Time Scrubber at bottom

**Design Intent:**
- Global KPIs always visible
- Click-to-zoom for detail exploration
- Dependencies are visual, not hidden in data

**Implementation:**
- Header: `apps/canvas/src/components/AppNavbar.tsx` (future)
- Zoom popup: Click handler → animated zoom transition
- Time Scrubber: `apps/canvas/src/components/TimeScrubber.tsx` (future)

---

### screen-3.jpg: Multi-Scale View (All 3 LODs)

![Multi-Scale View](screen-3.jpg)

**What it shows:**

| LOD Level | Time Scale | Visualization              | Planning Horizon |
|-----------|------------|----------------------------|------------------|
| **Macro** | 12 years   | Heatmap (density clusters) | Strategic        |
| **Meso**  | 2 years    | DNA strips (task blocks)   | Tactical         |
| **Micro** | 4 weeks    | Detailed Gantt (cards)     | Operational      |

- Task tooltip: "Duration: 8h | Resources: 2 Techs, Pit 3 | Cost: $1200"
- Time Scrubber spanning all views

**Design Intent:**
- Same data, different aggregations per zoom level
- Each LOD answers different planning questions
- Seamless transitions (no loading states)

**Implementation:**
- MacroLayer: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MacroLayer.tsx`
- MesoLayer: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MesoLayer.tsx`
- MicroLayer: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MicroLayer.tsx`

---

### screen-4.png: Campaign View with Conflict Alert

![Campaign View](screen-4.png)

**What it shows:**
- All 3 LOD views stacked
- Complex dependency arrows in Micro view
- Task grouping: "Task Group: Bogie Overhaul Campaign | T145, T146, T147"
- Resource detail: "8 Techs, Pit 1 & 2, Lift Crane"
- **Conflict Alert:** "Overlapping with Signaling Upgrade"

**Design Intent:**
- Tasks can be grouped into "Campaigns" (related work)
- Conflicts are explicit alerts, not just visual indicators
- Resources include equipment (Lift Crane), not just man-hours

**Data Implications:**
- Need `Campaign` entity grouping related tasks
- Need `Equipment` dimension (Pit, Lift Crane, Wheel Lathe)
- Conflict detection must span multiple constraint types

---

### screen-5.png: Meso View with Capacity Chart

![Capacity Chart](screen-5.png)

**What it shows:**
- Train IDs (T001-T050) on Y axis
- 2028-2029 timeline on X axis
- Diagonal task pattern (staggered scheduling)
- **Capacity Utilization Chart at bottom:**
  - Mechanical work (blue bars)
  - Electrical work (yellow bars)
  - Capacity Limit line
  - Utilization over time

**Design Intent:**
- Diagonal pattern = "Equalizing" (Tokyo Metro methodology)
- Capacity split by work type, not just total
- Visual correlation between schedule and capacity

**Data Implications:**
- Tasks need `workType: "mechanical" | "electrical"` attribute
- `FactResourceUsage` must track by type
- Capacity chart component synced with main timeline

**Implementation:**
- Capacity Chart: `apps/canvas/src/components/CapacityChart.tsx` (future)

---

### screen-6.png: Micro View with Pit Occupancy

![Pit Occupancy](screen-6.png)

**What it shows:**
- Header: "Fleet Avail: 99%", "Conflict Alerts: 0"
- Zoom Level: Micro (4 Weeks)
- Train rows with tasks and dependencies
- **Pit Occupancy Panel:**
  - Pit 1, Pit 2, Pit 3, Wash Bay rows
  - Tasks shown in allocated resource
  - **Red conflict indicator** on overlapping pit assignment
- Time scale selector: 1 | 2 | 4 | 6 Weeks

**Design Intent:**
- Pit allocation is first-class visualization
- Conflicts visible at resource level
- Flexible time window for operational planning

**Data Implications:**
- Need `FactPitOccupancy` or `pitAssignment` on tasks
- Wash Bay as separate resource type
- Conflict detection at pit level

**Implementation:**
- Pit Occupancy: `apps/canvas/src/components/PitOccupancy.tsx` (future)

---

### screen-7.png: Scenario Diff View

![Scenario Diff](screen-7.png)

**What it shows:**
- Title: "Advanced Gantt - Diff View: Scenario Comparison"
- Conflict zone: Red hatched area (Oct 1-3)
- Original task position: Red border (in conflict)
- Resolved task position: Blue (moved to Oct 6)
- Arrow showing movement from conflict → resolution
- Gray tasks: Unchanged in scenario

**Design Intent:**
- Visual diff more powerful than table diff
- Conflict zones shown as areas, not just task borders
- Resolution path is explicit (arrow shows "what changed")

**Color Coding:**
- Red hatched = Conflict zone
- Red border = Task in conflict
- Blue = Modified/resolved task
- Gray = Unchanged task

**Implementation:**
- Scenario comparison: `apps/canvas/src/components/ScenarioDiff.tsx` (future)

---

## Technical Requirements by Layer

### MacroLayer (zoom < 0.5x)

**Purpose:** Strategic 10-20 year overview

**Visual:** Heatmap with color gradient showing task density/health

**Technical:**
- Single `PlaneGeometry` covering visible area
- Fragment shader receiving `DataTexture`
- Color ramp: Yellow (low) → Orange → Red (high density)
- Target: 1 draw call

**Implementation:** `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MacroLayer.tsx`

---

### MesoLayer (0.5x ≤ zoom ≤ 2x)

**Purpose:** Tactical 2-5 year planning

**Visual:** DNA-like diagonal strips of task blocks

**Technical:**
- `InstancedMesh` with up to 60,000 instances
- Colors via `InstancedBufferAttribute`
- Spatial Hash Grid for hover detection
- Alternating row backgrounds

**Implementation:** `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MesoLayer.tsx`

---

### MicroLayer (zoom > 2x)

**Purpose:** Operational 4-8 week scheduling

**Visual:** Detailed task cards with labels, grid, dependencies

**Technical:**
- `<Html>` component from drei with `transform`
- `Line2` (Fat Lines) for dependency arrows
- BlueprintJS styling (Card, Tag, Intent)
- Accessibility: tabIndex, ARIA labels

**Implementation:** `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MicroLayer.tsx`

---

## Color Palette

### Task Colors

| Type | Color | Hex | Usage |
|------|-------|-----|-------|
| Routine/A-Check | Deep Blue | `#3b82f6` | Standard maintenance |
| Overhaul/D-Check | Orange | `#f59e0b` | Major overhauls |
| Inspection | Gray | `#6b7280` | Quick inspections |

### Semantic Colors

| Meaning | Color | Hex | BlueprintJS Intent |
|---------|-------|-----|-------------------|
| Conflict/Alert | Red | `#ef4444` | Intent.DANGER |
| Warning | Yellow | `#eab308` | Intent.WARNING |
| Success/Resolved | Green | `#22c55e` | Intent.SUCCESS |
| Neutral | Blue | `#3b82f6` | Intent.PRIMARY |

### UI Colors

| Element | Color | Hex |
|---------|-------|-----|
| Background | Soft gray | `#f4f4f5` (zinc-100) |
| Card background | White | `#ffffff` |
| Grid lines | Light gray | `#e5e7eb` |
| Dependency arrows | Slate | `#64748b` |

### Heatmap Gradient

```
Low density    →    Medium    →    High density
#fef3c7 (yellow)  #fb923c (orange)  #dc2626 (red)
```

---

## Design Principles

### 1. Progressive Disclosure via Zoom

Don't show everything at once. Zoom level determines information density.

| Zoom | Shows | Hides |
|------|-------|-------|
| Macro | Density patterns, hotspots | Individual tasks |
| Meso | Task blocks, patterns | Task details, labels |
| Micro | Full details, dependencies | Big picture |

### 2. Spatial Memory over Navigation

Users learn where things are. No page switches, just pan/zoom.

- Train T001 is always at the top
- 2025 is always to the left of 2026
- Context preserved during exploration

### 3. Conflicts are First-Class Citizens

Not hidden in tooltips or logs. Explicit visual treatment.

- Red indicators at task level
- Red hatched zones for conflict areas
- Alert badges in header
- Conflict explanation in tooltips

### 4. Resources are Visible

Pit occupancy shown, not just calculated internally.

- Capacity charts alongside schedule
- Equipment tracking (Wheel Lathe, Lift Crane)
- Utilization percentages in real-time

### 5. Scenarios are Comparable

Side-by-side or overlay comparison for What-If analysis.

- Diff view shows what changed
- Metrics delta (cost, conflicts, utilization)
- Visual path from problem → solution

---

## Implementation Status

### Completed (POC Validated)

- [x] Semantic zoom concept (canvas.html)
- [x] 3 LOD layers with smooth transitions
- [x] Pan/zoom navigation
- [x] Sidebar sync with canvas
- [x] Timeline header with adaptive granularity
- [x] Conflict indicators (red highlighting)

### In Progress (Production App)

- [x] F101: Vite + React 18 + BlueprintJS skeleton
- [x] F102: R3F Canvas + OrthographicCamera
- [x] F103: BlueprintJS layout (Navbar, Card sidebar)
- [ ] F104: Data types and mock generator ← **NEXT**
- [ ] F105-F117: Remaining features

### Future Features (Post-Demo)

- [ ] Scenario comparison (diff view)
- [ ] Pit Occupancy panel
- [ ] Capacity utilization chart
- [ ] Campaign grouping
- [ ] Solver integration
- [ ] Drag-and-drop rescheduling

---

## Open Questions

1. **Dependency arrows at Meso level?** May clutter, but shows relationships
2. **Capacity chart position?** Bottom (like screen-5) or collapsible panel?
3. **Pit Occupancy visibility?** Always visible or toggle?
4. **Conflict resolution UX?** Solver suggests, human approves?

---

*Last updated: 2025-12-15*
*Source: AI-generated reference images representing design vision*

# @mms/canvas

Production application for the Metro Integrated Planning System - a high-performance "Semantic Zoom" visualization for railway maintenance planning.

## Quick Start

```bash
# From this directory
bun run dev

# Or from root
cd ../.. && bun run dev --filter=@mms/canvas
```

The app runs at http://localhost:5173

## Tech Stack

| Technology | Version | Purpose |
|------------|---------|---------|
| **Vite** | 5.x | Build tool and dev server |
| **React** | 18.3.1 | UI framework (required for BlueprintJS 6) |
| **BlueprintJS** | 6.1.0 | UI component library (Palantir) |
| **R3F** | 8.x | React Three Fiber for WebGL |
| **drei** | 9.x | R3F helpers and components |
| **Three.js** | 0.170.x | 3D rendering engine |

## Project Structure

```
src/
├── main.tsx              # Entry point
├── App.tsx               # Main component with layout
├── components/           # BlueprintJS UI components
│   ├── AppLayout.tsx         # Navbar, Sidebar, Canvas container
│   ├── TrainSidebar.tsx      # Train IDs (Card)
│   ├── ZoomControls.tsx      # ButtonGroup +/-
│   └── LODIndicator.tsx      # Tag component
├── canvas/               # R3F rendering
│   ├── InfiniteCanvas.tsx
│   ├── CameraController.tsx
│   └── layers/
│       ├── MacroLayer.tsx    # Heatmap (InstancedMesh/Shader)
│       ├── MesoLayer.tsx     # Task blocks (InstancedMesh)
│       └── MicroLayer.tsx    # Detailed cards (Html)
├── data/
│   ├── types.ts              # Train, Task interfaces
│   └── generator.ts          # Mock data generation
└── hooks/
    └── useViewport.ts        # zoom, pan, LOD state
```

## LOD System (Level of Detail)

The canvas implements three rendering layers based on camera zoom:

| LOD | Zoom | Visualization | Technical |
|-----|------|---------------|-----------|
| **Macro** | < 0.5x | Heatmap (yellow→orange→red) | InstancedMesh or Shader |
| **Meso** | 0.5-2.0x | Task blocks (DNA pattern) | InstancedMesh |
| **Micro** | > 2.0x | Detailed task cards | drei Html + CSS |

## Features (see features.json)

- **F101**: Vite + React 18 + BlueprintJS skeleton ✅
- **F102-F103**: R3F Canvas + Camera setup
- **F104-F107**: Data, MesoLayer, pan/zoom, LOD
- **F108-F110**: MacroLayer, MicroLayer, transitions
- **F111-F114**: Sidebar, timeline, controls
- **F115-F117**: Instructions, performance, visual parity

## Commands

```bash
bun run dev        # Start dev server
bun run build      # Production build
bun run preview    # Preview production build
bun run typecheck  # Type check
```

## Visual Reference

See `../../design/` for pixel-perfect target specifications.

## Related

- **POC Reference**: `../../design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html` - Visual/algorithm reference
- **Domain Docs**: `../../docs/DOMAIN.md` - Fleet, maintenance types
- **UX Spec**: `../../docs/UX_SPECIFICATION.md` - Interaction patterns

# Metro Integrated Planning System (Monorepo)

High-Performance Enterprise Integrated Planning System with "Semantic Zoom" UX (Google Earth-style).

## Commands

```bash
bun i              # Install dependencies
bun run dev        # Start development server
bun run build      # Production build
bun run typecheck  # Type check all packages
bun run lint       # Lint all packages
bun run test       # Run tests
```

## Tech Stack

- **Runtime:** Bun
- **Monorepo:** Turborepo
- **Build Tool:** Vite 5.x
- **Frontend:** React 18.3.1 (required for BlueprintJS 6)
- **UI Components:** BlueprintJS 6.1.0 (Palantir)
- **Graphics Engine:** @react-three/fiber 8.x, @react-three/drei 9.x, three 0.170.x
- **State Management:** Custom state-machine (packages/toolkit)

## Project Structure

```
system/
├── apps/canvas/          # Production React app (Vite + R3F + BlueprintJS)
├── packages/
│   └── toolkit/          # State machine, utilities
├── URGENT_POC/           # Archived POC (reference only)
└── design/               # Visual reference specifications
```

## Visual References (design/)

The `design/` directory contains the visual specifications that this system must replicate. See [design/README.md](./design/README.md) for detailed mapping between images and implementation layers.

| Image | View | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| screen-1.png | Macro | Strategic 10-20 year heatmap overview |
| screen-2.png | Meso | Tactical DNA-like allocation strips |
| screen-3.jpg | Micro | Operational task cards with dependencies |
| screen-4.png | Meso | Dense diagonal allocation pattern |
| screen-5.png | Meso | Color-coded resource allocation |
| screen-6.png | Micro | Task details with conflict indicators |
| screen-7.png | UI | Header/sidebar layout reference |

## LOD System (Level of Detail)

The system implements three rendering layers based on camera zoom:

- **Macro (zoom < 0.5):** Procedural shader heatmap - 1 draw call
- **Meso (0.5 ≤ zoom ≤ 2.0):** InstancedMesh with 60k instances
- **Micro (zoom > 2.0):** HTML overlay with Line2 dependency curves

## Documentation

- `docs/` - Domain documentation (requirements, UX specs, research)
- `agent_docs/` - Technical documentation for AI agents
- `design/` - Visual reference specifications

---

# Harness Protocol for Long-Running AI Agents

## What is a Harness?

A **harness** is a framework that enables AI agents to work effectively on complex tasks that span multiple sessions. Created by **Anthropic** as part of the Claude Agent SDK (formerly Claude Code SDK), the harness addresses a fundamental challenge:

> "The core challenge of long-running agents is that they must work in discrete sessions, and each new session begins with no memory of what came before."

The harness acts as a **coordination layer** wrapped around ordinary Claude sessions. It tracks files, tasks, and test results across hours of execution, spinning up fresh conversations whenever one thread gets too bloated to stay coherent.

## Why Anthropic Created the Harness

After more than six months shipping updates to Claude Code, Anthropic solved hard problems:
- How agents should manage **memory across long-running tasks**
- How to handle **permission systems** that balance autonomy with user control
- How to **coordinate subagents** working toward a shared goal

The key insight was finding a way for agents to quickly understand the state of work when starting with a fresh context window.

## Core Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Harness Framework                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐  │
│  │  Initializer │ → │    Coding    │ → │    Coding    │  │
│  │    Agent     │    │    Agent     │    │    Agent     │  │
│  │  (Session 1) │    │  (Session 2) │    │  (Session N) │  │
│  └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘  │
│         │                   │                   │           │
│         └───────────────────┼───────────────────┘           │
│                             │                               │
│                             ▼                               │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │              Shared State Files                       │  │
│  │  • claude-progress.txt  (session history)            │  │
│  │  • features.json        (source of truth)            │  │
│  │  • CLAUDE.md            (project context)            │  │
│  │  • git history          (code changes)               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Two-Agent Architecture

Anthropic recommends a **two-fold approach**:

### 1. Initializer Agent (First Session)
- Sets up the initial environment
- Creates the harness files (features.json, claude-progress.txt)
- Establishes project structure
- Runs on a specialized prompt optimized for setup

### 2. Coding Agent (Subsequent Sessions)
- Makes incremental progress on features
- Leaves artifacts for the next session
- Maintains clean code suitable for merging
- Commits progress to git with descriptive messages

## Core Files

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `CLAUDE.md` | Project context, instructions, and conventions |
| `claude-progress.txt` | Session history and current state |
| `features.json` | Feature requirements with pass/fail status (source of truth) |
| `agent_docs/` | Additional documentation for the agent |
| `init.sh` | Script to start dev server and run basic tests |
| `CLAUDE_NOTES.md` | Learnings and gotchas for future sessions |

## The Work Loop

When a session starts, the agent follows this loop:

```
1. READ claude-progress.txt
   → Understand current state and what was done before

2. READ features.json
   → Identify next feature to implement (passes: false)

3. RUN init.sh
   → Start dev server, verify app is in working state

4. IMPLEMENT one feature at a time
   → Focus on single feature, don't try to do everything

5. TEST the feature
   → Verify it works as specified in steps

6. COMMIT changes
   → Granular commits with descriptive messages

7. UPDATE features.json
   → Mark feature as passes: true

8. UPDATE claude-progress.txt
   → Log what was done for next session

9. REPEAT or STOP
   → Continue to next feature or end session
```

## Critical AI Limitations & Mitigations

> **IMPORTANT**: AI agents have fundamental limitations that this harness addresses.

### The Blind Spot Problem

**AI cannot see what it creates.** When building UI/apps, the AI:
- Cannot see the rendered output
- Often assumes blank screens are "working" because code compiled
- Marks features as complete based on code logic, not visual verification
- May claim success when the app shows nothing or errors

**Solution**: Mandatory visual testing with MCP after every feature.

### The Stale Knowledge Problem

**AI training data is outdated.** Claude's knowledge cutoff means:
- Library APIs may have changed
- Configuration patterns may be deprecated
- New versions have different syntax
- Best practices may have evolved

**Solution**: Always check the current date and search for updated documentation.

### The Guessing Problem

**AI tends to guess instead of research.** When stuck, AI will:
- Try random solutions repeatedly
- Waste context on failed attempts
- Not realize when it's going in circles

**Solution**: Mandatory research protocol for non-obvious errors.

---

## Best Practices

### 1. One Feature at a Time
Don't attempt to complete everything at once. Focus on a single feature, test it, commit it, then move to the next.

### 2. Clean State Between Sessions
Leave the environment in a clean state:
- No major bugs
- Well-documented code
- Orderly structure
- Tests passing

### 3. features.json is Source of Truth
Never declare the project complete without all features passing. The `passes` field should only change from `false` to `true` after verification.

### 4. Commit After Each Feature
Maintain granular git history. Each feature should have its own commit(s).

### 5. Research Before Guessing
When encountering errors, classify them:
- **Simple errors** (typos, syntax): Fix directly
- **Complex errors** (runtime, config): Research online first

### 6. Test Before Marking Complete
Always verify the feature works before marking `passes: true`.

### 7. ALWAYS Check Current Date
Before starting work, verify the current date. **Do NOT assume you are in the year of your training data.** Use the system date to ensure you search for current documentation.

### 8. Install Dependencies via Commands
**NEVER manually edit package.json/Cargo.toml/requirements.txt to add dependencies.**

```bash
# CORRECT - Let package manager resolve versions
bun add react
npm install express
pip install requests
cargo add serde

# WRONG - Manual version specification
# Editing package.json: "react": "^18.0.0"  <- DON'T DO THIS
```

Why: Package managers install the latest stable compatible versions and handle peer dependencies correctly.

---

## MCP Visual Testing (MANDATORY)

### Why Visual Testing is Required

AI agents **cannot see rendered UI**. This creates a critical gap:

```
What AI knows:          What actually happens:
✓ Code compiled         ✗ Blank white screen
✓ No TypeScript errors  ✗ React error boundary
✓ Tests passed          ✗ Component doesn't render
✓ Server running        ✗ CSS completely broken
```

**Without visual verification, AI will mark broken features as complete.**

### Visual Testing Protocol

**See CLAUDE.md section "MCP VISUAL TESTING (MANDATORY)"** for the complete protocol.

Summary: Use **Playwright MCP** (headless, does not steal focus):

```
1. mcp__playwright__browser_navigate url="..."
2. mcp__playwright__browser_console_messages level="error"
3. mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot filename="..."
4. Analyze screenshot
5. mcp__playwright__browser_close
```

---

## Error Research Protocol

### When to Research vs. Fix Directly

| Error Type | Example | Action |
|------------|---------|--------|
| **Obvious** | Typo in variable name | Fix directly |
| **Syntax** | Missing semicolon | Fix directly |
| **Import** | Wrong import path | Fix directly |
| **Config** | Webpack/Vite config | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
| **Runtime** | Module not found | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
| **Library** | API changed | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
| **Unknown** | Cryptic error | **RESEARCH FIRST** |

### The STOP-SEARCH-UNDERSTAND-APPLY Method

When encountering non-obvious errors:

```
1. STOP
   - Do NOT try random solutions
   - Do NOT keep making changes hoping something works
   - Recognize when you're stuck

2. SEARCH
   - WebSearch: "[error message] + [technology] + [current year]"
   - WebFetch: Read official documentation
   - Include the CURRENT YEAR in searches (not your training year)

3. UNDERSTAND
   - Read the search results carefully
   - Identify the ROOT CAUSE
   - Understand WHY the error occurs

4. APPLY
   - Implement the CORRECT solution
   - Not a workaround or hack
   - The solution from documentation

5. DOCUMENT
   - Add to CLAUDE_NOTES.md if it's a recurring issue
   - Note in claude-progress.txt
```

### Example: Research Flow

```
Error: "Module '@tauri-apps/api/fs' not found"

❌ WRONG approach:
   → Try different import paths randomly
   → Downgrade versions blindly
   → Copy code from training data (outdated)

✅ CORRECT approach:
   1. STOP - This is a config/library error
   2. SEARCH - "tauri 2.0 fs module not found 2025"
   3. UNDERSTAND - Tauri 2.0 moved fs to plugin system
   4. APPLY - Install @tauri-apps/plugin-fs, update imports
   5. DOCUMENT - Note in CLAUDE_NOTES.md
```

---

## Session Startup Sequence

Every coding agent session should begin with:

```bash
# 1. Confirm working directory
pwd

# 2. Read progress to understand state
cat claude-progress.txt

# 3. Check recent git history
git log --oneline -10

# 4. Read feature requirements
cat features.json

# 5. Start development server
./init.sh

# 6. Verify app is working
# (run basic tests or visual check)

# 7. Begin work on next feature
```

## Context Management

The Claude Agent SDK implements automatic **compaction** when context limit approaches. However, you can help by:

- Using `/clear` between unrelated tasks
- Delegating research to subagents
- Keeping CLAUDE.md concise
- Writing clear, focused commit messages

## Results

Using this harness approach, Anthropic has observed:
- Claude maintaining focus for **30+ hours** on complex tasks
- Agents completing multi-week projects across sessions
- Consistent quality without context degradation

## Official Sources

- [Effective harnesses for long-running agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents)
- [Building agents with the Claude Agent SDK](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-agents-with-the-claude-agent-sdk)
- [Claude Code: Best practices for agentic coding](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices)

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Visual Design Specifications

This directory contains the **Visual Reference Images** for the Metro Integrated Planning System.

> **Important:** These images are AI-generated and represent the VISION, not pixel-perfect specifications.
> They capture the essence of what we're building: a "Single Pane of Glass" with semantic zoom.

---

## Core Design Vision

### The "80% Cognitive Load Reduction" Goal

The system aims to reduce analysis and planning overhead by 80%+ through:

| Principle                   | Description                                | Implementation                  |
|-----------------------------|--------------------------------------------|---------------------------------|
| **Single Pane of Glass**    | Everything in one view, no page navigation | Pan/zoom instead of tabs        |
| **Semantic Zoom**           | Context changes with zoom level            | 3 LOD layers (Macro/Meso/Micro) |
| **Direct Manipulation**     | Drag to reschedule, not forms              | Future: drag-and-drop tasks     |
| **Game-like Visualization** | WebGL beyond standard charts               | R3F with custom shaders         |

### Design Inspirations

- **Google Earth/Maps:** Seamless zoom from planet to street
- **Strategy Games:** Information density that doesn't overwhelm (Factorio, Cities Skylines)
- **Financial Terminals:** Dense data, clear hierarchy (Bloomberg Terminal)

---

## Screen Reference Analysis

### screen-1.png: Simulation Setup

![Simulation Setup](screen-1.png)

**What it shows:**
- Maintenance Heatmap in background
- Modal: "Simulation Setup: Scenario A"
- Constraint sliders (Labor Availability: 50%)
- Date range selector
- Infrastructure toggle (Maintenance Pit Status)
- "Run Solver (Est. 45s)" button

**Design Intent:**
- What-If scenarios start with constraint adjustment
- Solver is async with time estimate
- Simple controls for complex simulations

**Implementation:** Future feature (Scenario Mode)

---

### screen-2.png: Full System with Zoom Popup

![Full System](screen-2.png)

**What it shows:**
- Header KPIs: "Fleet Avail: 98%", "Annual Budget: On Track"
- Maintenance Heatmap (2021-2022+)
- Legend: Routine maintenance, Mid-life overhauls, Capacity bottlenecks
- Zoom popup: "Zoom in 4 weeks" detail view
- Tasks with durations: "Brake Rev. T104 (8h)", "Wheel Lathe T112 (4h)"
- Dependency arrows between tasks
- Time Scrubber at bottom

**Design Intent:**
- Global KPIs always visible
- Click-to-zoom for detail exploration
- Dependencies are visual, not hidden in data

**Implementation:**
- Header: `apps/canvas/src/components/AppNavbar.tsx` (future)
- Zoom popup: Click handler → animated zoom transition
- Time Scrubber: `apps/canvas/src/components/TimeScrubber.tsx` (future)

---

### screen-3.jpg: Multi-Scale View (All 3 LODs)

![Multi-Scale View](screen-3.jpg)

**What it shows:**

| LOD Level | Time Scale | Visualization              | Planning Horizon |
|-----------|------------|----------------------------|------------------|
| **Macro** | 12 years   | Heatmap (density clusters) | Strategic        |
| **Meso**  | 2 years    | DNA strips (task blocks)   | Tactical         |
| **Micro** | 4 weeks    | Detailed Gantt (cards)     | Operational      |

- Task tooltip: "Duration: 8h | Resources: 2 Techs, Pit 3 | Cost: $1200"
- Time Scrubber spanning all views

**Design Intent:**
- Same data, different aggregations per zoom level
- Each LOD answers different planning questions
- Seamless transitions (no loading states)

**Implementation:**
- MacroLayer: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MacroLayer.tsx`
- MesoLayer: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MesoLayer.tsx`
- MicroLayer: `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MicroLayer.tsx`

---

### screen-4.png: Campaign View with Conflict Alert

![Campaign View](screen-4.png)

**What it shows:**
- All 3 LOD views stacked
- Complex dependency arrows in Micro view
- Task grouping: "Task Group: Bogie Overhaul Campaign | T145, T146, T147"
- Resource detail: "8 Techs, Pit 1 & 2, Lift Crane"
- **Conflict Alert:** "Overlapping with Signaling Upgrade"

**Design Intent:**
- Tasks can be grouped into "Campaigns" (related work)
- Conflicts are explicit alerts, not just visual indicators
- Resources include equipment (Lift Crane), not just man-hours

**Data Implications:**
- Need `Campaign` entity grouping related tasks
- Need `Equipment` dimension (Pit, Lift Crane, Wheel Lathe)
- Conflict detection must span multiple constraint types

---

### screen-5.png: Meso View with Capacity Chart

![Capacity Chart](screen-5.png)

**What it shows:**
- Train IDs (T001-T050) on Y axis
- 2028-2029 timeline on X axis
- Diagonal task pattern (staggered scheduling)
- **Capacity Utilization Chart at bottom:**
  - Mechanical work (blue bars)
  - Electrical work (yellow bars)
  - Capacity Limit line
  - Utilization over time

**Design Intent:**
- Diagonal pattern = "Equalizing" (Tokyo Metro methodology)
- Capacity split by work type, not just total
- Visual correlation between schedule and capacity

**Data Implications:**
- Tasks need `workType: "mechanical" | "electrical"` attribute
- `FactResourceUsage` must track by type
- Capacity chart component synced with main timeline

**Implementation:**
- Capacity Chart: `apps/canvas/src/components/CapacityChart.tsx` (future)

---

### screen-6.png: Micro View with Pit Occupancy

![Pit Occupancy](screen-6.png)

**What it shows:**
- Header: "Fleet Avail: 99%", "Conflict Alerts: 0"
- Zoom Level: Micro (4 Weeks)
- Train rows with tasks and dependencies
- **Pit Occupancy Panel:**
  - Pit 1, Pit 2, Pit 3, Wash Bay rows
  - Tasks shown in allocated resource
  - **Red conflict indicator** on overlapping pit assignment
- Time scale selector: 1 | 2 | 4 | 6 Weeks

**Design Intent:**
- Pit allocation is first-class visualization
- Conflicts visible at resource level
- Flexible time window for operational planning

**Data Implications:**
- Need `FactPitOccupancy` or `pitAssignment` on tasks
- Wash Bay as separate resource type
- Conflict detection at pit level

**Implementation:**
- Pit Occupancy: `apps/canvas/src/components/PitOccupancy.tsx` (future)

---

### screen-7.png: Scenario Diff View

![Scenario Diff](screen-7.png)

**What it shows:**
- Title: "Advanced Gantt - Diff View: Scenario Comparison"
- Conflict zone: Red hatched area (Oct 1-3)
- Original task position: Red border (in conflict)
- Resolved task position: Blue (moved to Oct 6)
- Arrow showing movement from conflict → resolution
- Gray tasks: Unchanged in scenario

**Design Intent:**
- Visual diff more powerful than table diff
- Conflict zones shown as areas, not just task borders
- Resolution path is explicit (arrow shows "what changed")

**Color Coding:**
- Red hatched = Conflict zone
- Red border = Task in conflict
- Blue = Modified/resolved task
- Gray = Unchanged task

**Implementation:**
- Scenario comparison: `apps/canvas/src/components/ScenarioDiff.tsx` (future)

---

## Technical Requirements by Layer

### MacroLayer (zoom < 0.5x)

**Purpose:** Strategic 10-20 year overview

**Visual:** Heatmap with color gradient showing task density/health

**Technical:**
- Single `PlaneGeometry` covering visible area
- Fragment shader receiving `DataTexture`
- Color ramp: Yellow (low) → Orange → Red (high density)
- Target: 1 draw call

**Implementation:** `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MacroLayer.tsx`

---

### MesoLayer (0.5x ≤ zoom ≤ 2x)

**Purpose:** Tactical 2-5 year planning

**Visual:** DNA-like diagonal strips of task blocks

**Technical:**
- `InstancedMesh` with up to 60,000 instances
- Colors via `InstancedBufferAttribute`
- Spatial Hash Grid for hover detection
- Alternating row backgrounds

**Implementation:** `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MesoLayer.tsx`

---

### MicroLayer (zoom > 2x)

**Purpose:** Operational 4-8 week scheduling

**Visual:** Detailed task cards with labels, grid, dependencies

**Technical:**
- `<Html>` component from drei with `transform`
- `Line2` (Fat Lines) for dependency arrows
- BlueprintJS styling (Card, Tag, Intent)
- Accessibility: tabIndex, ARIA labels

**Implementation:** `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MicroLayer.tsx`

---

## Color Palette

### Task Colors

| Type | Color | Hex | Usage |
|------|-------|-----|-------|
| Routine/A-Check | Deep Blue | `#3b82f6` | Standard maintenance |
| Overhaul/D-Check | Orange | `#f59e0b` | Major overhauls |
| Inspection | Gray | `#6b7280` | Quick inspections |

### Semantic Colors

| Meaning | Color | Hex | BlueprintJS Intent |
|---------|-------|-----|-------------------|
| Conflict/Alert | Red | `#ef4444` | Intent.DANGER |
| Warning | Yellow | `#eab308` | Intent.WARNING |
| Success/Resolved | Green | `#22c55e` | Intent.SUCCESS |
| Neutral | Blue | `#3b82f6` | Intent.PRIMARY |

### UI Colors

| Element | Color | Hex |
|---------|-------|-----|
| Background | Soft gray | `#f4f4f5` (zinc-100) |
| Card background | White | `#ffffff` |
| Grid lines | Light gray | `#e5e7eb` |
| Dependency arrows | Slate | `#64748b` |

### Heatmap Gradient

```
Low density    →    Medium    →    High density
#fef3c7 (yellow)  #fb923c (orange)  #dc2626 (red)
```

---

## Design Principles

### 1. Progressive Disclosure via Zoom

Don't show everything at once. Zoom level determines information density.

| Zoom | Shows | Hides |
|------|-------|-------|
| Macro | Density patterns, hotspots | Individual tasks |
| Meso | Task blocks, patterns | Task details, labels |
| Micro | Full details, dependencies | Big picture |

### 2. Spatial Memory over Navigation

Users learn where things are. No page switches, just pan/zoom.

- Train T001 is always at the top
- 2025 is always to the left of 2026
- Context preserved during exploration

### 3. Conflicts are First-Class Citizens

Not hidden in tooltips or logs. Explicit visual treatment.

- Red indicators at task level
- Red hatched zones for conflict areas
- Alert badges in header
- Conflict explanation in tooltips

### 4. Resources are Visible

Pit occupancy shown, not just calculated internally.

- Capacity charts alongside schedule
- Equipment tracking (Wheel Lathe, Lift Crane)
- Utilization percentages in real-time

### 5. Scenarios are Comparable

Side-by-side or overlay comparison for What-If analysis.

- Diff view shows what changed
- Metrics delta (cost, conflicts, utilization)
- Visual path from problem → solution

---

## Implementation Status

### Completed (POC Validated)

- [x] Semantic zoom concept (canvas.html)
- [x] 3 LOD layers with smooth transitions
- [x] Pan/zoom navigation
- [x] Sidebar sync with canvas
- [x] Timeline header with adaptive granularity
- [x] Conflict indicators (red highlighting)

### In Progress (Production App)

- [x] F101: Vite + React 18 + BlueprintJS skeleton
- [x] F102: R3F Canvas + OrthographicCamera
- [x] F103: BlueprintJS layout (Navbar, Card sidebar)
- [ ] F104: Data types and mock generator ← **NEXT**
- [ ] F105-F117: Remaining features

### Future Features (Post-Demo)

- [ ] Scenario comparison (diff view)
- [ ] Pit Occupancy panel
- [ ] Capacity utilization chart
- [ ] Campaign grouping
- [ ] Solver integration
- [ ] Drag-and-drop rescheduling

---

## Open Questions

1. **Dependency arrows at Meso level?** May clutter, but shows relationships
2. **Capacity chart position?** Bottom (like screen-5) or collapsible panel?
3. **Pit Occupancy visibility?** Always visible or toggle?
4. **Conflict resolution UX?** Solver suggests, human approves?

---

*Last updated: 2025-12-15*
*Source: AI-generated reference images representing design vision*

- Conflict visual indicators

# MMS System Documentation

This directory contains domain knowledge, requirements, research, and UX specifications for the Metro Integrated Planning System.

## Document Index

| Document | Description | Audience |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| [DOMAIN.md](./DOMAIN.md) | Fleet composition, maintenance types, domain entities | Developers, Domain Experts |
| [REQUIREMENTS.md](./REQUIREMENTS.md) | Business requirements (from RFQ) | Product, Engineering |
| [UX_SPECIFICATION.md](./UX_SPECIFICATION.md) | UX design and interaction patterns | Designers, Frontend Devs |
| [RESEARCH.md](./RESEARCH.md) | Industry research on maintenance scheduling | All stakeholders |

## Project Context

### What is MMS?

The **Metro Integrated Planning System (MMS)** is a high-performance visualization tool for railway rolling stock maintenance planning. It provides a "Single Pane of Glass" solution that unifies:

- **Strategic Planning** (1-12 years): Budget allocation, major overhauls
- **Tactical Planning** (weeks-months): Workload balancing, resource leveling
- **Operational Planning** (daily): Run assignments, track allocation

### The "Semantic Zoom" Concept

Inspired by Google Earth, the system implements **semantic zoom** - the visualization automatically transforms based on zoom level:

| Zoom Level | Threshold | Time Scale | Visualization |
|------------|-----------|------------|---------------|
| Macro | < 0.5x | 10-20 years | Heatmap (yellow→orange→red) |
| Meso | 0.5-2x | 2-5 years | Task blocks (DNA pattern) |
| Micro | > 2x | 4-8 weeks | Detailed cards with labels |

### Current Implementation

**Production:** `apps/canvas` - React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS 6 app with semantic zoom.

```bash
# Run production app (http://localhost:5173)
cd apps/canvas && bun run dev
```

**Production Features (F101-F117):**

| Phase | Features | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| **Foundation** | F101-F103 | Vite + React 18 + BlueprintJS + R3F setup |
| **Core Rendering** | F104-F107 | Data, MesoLayer, pan/zoom, LOD state |
| **All Layers** | F108-F110 | MacroLayer, MicroLayer, smooth transitions |
| **UI Integration** | F111-F114 | Sidebar, timeline, controls, conflicts |
| **Polish** | F115-F117 | Instructions, performance, visual parity |

**POC Reference:**

The POC (`design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html`) serves as visual and algorithmic reference:
- Pan/zoom navigation, LOD transitions
- Macro/Meso/Micro layers
- Sidebar and timeline sync
- Conflict indicators

See `features.json` for complete feature list and status.

## Relationship to Implementation

### Production App (`apps/canvas`)

The UX patterns in [UX_SPECIFICATION.md](./UX_SPECIFICATION.md) are implemented in:
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/` - R3F rendering components
- `apps/canvas/src/components/` - BlueprintJS UI components
- `apps/canvas/src/hooks/` - State management (useViewport)

### Domain Entities

The domain entities in [DOMAIN.md](./DOMAIN.md) are implemented in:
- `apps/canvas/src/data/types.ts` - TypeScript interfaces
- `apps/canvas/src/data/generator.ts` - Mock data generation

### POC Reference

The validated prototype (`design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html`) serves as reference:
- Demonstrates semantic zoom concept
- Visual reference for production implementation
- Algorithm reference for LOD, heatmap, rendering logic

### Future Development

The full RFQ requirements in [REQUIREMENTS.md](./REQUIREMENTS.md) include features not yet implemented:
- Real-time mileage data integration
- What-If scenario simulation
- Cost forecasting and budget tracking
- IBM Maximo integration

These represent the product roadmap beyond the current demo.

## Quick Links

- **Technical Architecture**: See `agent_docs/architecture.md`
- **Visual References**: See `design/README.md`
- **Code Conventions**: See `agent_docs/conventions.md`
- **Session Progress**: See `claude-progress.txt`

See `features.json` for complete feature list and status.

# Metro Integrated Planning System (MMS)

## Project Overview
High-Performance Enterprise Integrated Planning System with "Semantic Zoom" UX (Google Earth-style) for metro fleet management. Renders 50+ trains and 10 years of tasks at constant 60fps using WebGL.

---

## HARNESS: Fundamental Concept (READ THIS FIRST)

> **This project uses a Harness.** If you don't know what that is, STOP and research:
> - [Effective harnesses for long-running agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents)

### What is a Harness?

A **Harness** is a framework that enables AI agents to work on long tasks across multiple context windows/sessions.

**Problem:** Each new Claude Code session starts with NO memory of previous sessions.

**Solution:** State files persist context between sessions:
- `claude-progress.txt` → session history
- `features.json` → requirements with status (passes: true/false)

### Work Protocol

```
1. READ claude-progress.txt (understand current state)
2. READ features.json (find next feature)
3. IMPLEMENT one feature
4. TEST visually (Playwright MCP)
5. COMMIT after passing
6. UPDATE claude-progress.txt
7. STOP (leave codebase production-ready)
```

### Self-Correction Protocol

If you don't understand what harness is after reading this:
1. **SEARCH:** WebSearch "Anthropic harness agents 2025"
2. **ANALYZE:** Why was local documentation unclear?
3. **FIX:** Update CLAUDE.md or hooks to improve clarity
4. **DOCUMENT:** Record the fix in claude-progress.txt

### Harness Files

| File                  | Purpose              | When to Read       |
|-----------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| `claude-progress.txt` | Session history      | **ALWAYS first**   |
| `features.json`       | Features with status | After progress.txt |
| `CLAUDE.md`           | Project rules        | Reference          |

---

## KISS Philosophy

**KISS is NOT "do the minimum". It IS "don't add complexity that blocks progress".**

The goal is **speed of delivery with quality**. The product must impress the client to win contracts.

### What KISS means (examples)

| ✅ DO                                                         | ❌ DON'T                                                          |
|--------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Add polish, debug tools, UX refinements that accelerate work | Spend days on infrastructure before having something presentable |
| Use best practices that prevent future problems              | Add abstractions for hypothetical future needs                   |
| Preserve knowledge/tips for when they become relevant        | Ignore proven patterns just because "not needed yet"             |

### When to Increase Complexity (examples)

| ✅ Increase complexity when...     | ❌ Do NOT increase when... |
|-----------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Current approach blocks a feature | "It would be cleaner"     |
| Performance is unacceptable       | "Best practices say so"   |
| Code duplication becomes painful  | Premature optimization    |
| Team needs shared abstractions    | Speculative future needs  |

### Example: POC-first approach

When the monorepo setup consumed days debugging config, a pure HTML POC (`design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html`) was created to validate the concept quickly.

**Lesson:** Start simple, validate fast, then add complexity incrementally.

---

## Production App: apps/canvas

The production application is located at `apps/canvas/` - a React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS 6 app that implements the "Semantic Zoom" UX concept.

### Product Entry Point

**`apps/canvas`** - The production React app with semantic zoom.

```bash
# Run development server (http://localhost:5173)
cd apps/canvas && bun run dev

# Or from root
bun run dev --filter=@mms/canvas
```

### Tech Stack

| Technology    | Version | Purpose                                   |
|---------------|---------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Vite**      | 5.x     | Build tool and dev server                 |
| **React**     | 18.3.1  | UI framework (required for BlueprintJS 6) |
| **BlueprintJS** | 6.1.0 | UI component library                      |
| **R3F**       | 8.x     | React Three Fiber for WebGL               |
| **drei**      | 9.x     | R3F helpers and components                |
| **Three.js**  | 0.170.x | 3D rendering engine                       |

### Features

| Feature       | Description                                         | LOD Level |
|---------------|-----------------------------------------------------|-----------|
| **Pan**       | Drag to navigate through time (X) and trains (Y)    | All       |
| **Zoom**      | Scroll to zoom in/out, zoom centers on cursor       | All       |
| **Macro View** | Heatmap showing task density (yellow→orange→red)   | < 0.5x    |
| **Meso View** | DNA-like task strips with colored blocks            | 0.5x - 2x |
| **Micro View** | Detailed task cards with labels and grid           | > 2x      |
| **Sidebar**   | Train IDs synced with vertical pan (BlueprintJS Card) | All     |
| **Timeline**  | Date labels with adaptive granularity               | All       |

### Roadmap

See `features.json` for complete feature list. Key phases:

```
✅ F001-F015:  POC validation (archived)
🔄 F101-F103:  Foundation - Vite + React 18 + BlueprintJS + R3F ← CURRENT
⏳ F104-F107:  Core rendering - Data, MesoLayer, pan/zoom, LOD
⏳ F108-F110:  All LOD layers - Macro, Micro, transitions
⏳ F111-F114:  UI integration - Sidebar, timeline, controls
⏳ F115-F117:  Polish - Instructions, performance, visual parity
```

---

## POC Reference

A standalone HTML demo exists for visual and algorithmic reference:

**`design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html`** - Validated prototype.

```bash
# View the POC reference
open design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html    # macOS
```

Use as reference for:
- Visual appearance of semantic zoom
- LOD algorithms (Macro/Meso/Micro)
- Rendering logic (heatmap, task blocks, cards)

---

### Key Rule

**Do NOT write throwaway code without clear time/agility gains.** If code will be rewritten anyway, the simple version must provide faster iteration or validation—otherwise, just build it right the first time.
**If you know something will cause problems later (performance, UX, maintainability), address it now based on knowledge and testing—not fear, not speculation.**

---

## Documentation Rules

- **Language:** Always write documentation in English
- **Location:** Prefer updating existing docs over creating new files
- **Conciseness:** Keep docs actionable, not verbose

---

## Tech Stack

### Production App (apps/canvas)
- **Build Tool:** Vite 5.x
- **Frontend:** React 18.3.1 (required for BlueprintJS 6)
- **UI Components:** BlueprintJS 6.1.0 (Palantir)
- **Graphics Engine:** @react-three/fiber 8.x, @react-three/drei 9.x, three 0.170.x
- **State Management:** Custom state-machine (packages/toolkit)

### Monorepo Infrastructure
- **Runtime:** Bun 1.2.0
- **Monorepo:** Turborepo
- **Shared Package:** @mms/toolkit (React 18 || 19 compatible)

## Quick Commands

> ⚠️ **ALWAYS run `./init.sh --no-server` first** to verify environment.

### Production App (apps/canvas)

```bash
# 1. Initialize and verify environment
./init.sh --no-server

# 2. Install dependencies
bun i

# 3. Run production app (http://localhost:5173)
cd apps/canvas && bun run dev

# 4. Build for production
cd apps/canvas && bun run build

# 5. Type check
bun run typecheck
```

### POC Reference

```bash
# View the POC (reference only)
open design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html
```

### Troubleshooting

This is a **monorepo**. If root scripts fail, run directly in the app:

```bash
# Direct command (always works)
cd apps/canvas && bun run dev

# Check what scripts exist
cat apps/canvas/package.json | grep -A10 '"scripts"'
```

---

## CRITICAL AI RULES

> **READ THIS FIRST.** These rules address fundamental AI limitations.

### Rule 1: CHECK THE CURRENT DATE

**Your training data is outdated.** Before starting ANY work:

```bash
date  # Check actual current date
```

**DO NOT assume the current year is your training year.** If your training was in 2024 but it's now 2025:
- Documentation has changed
- Library versions have changed
- APIs have changed
- Best practices have evolved

**Always include the CURRENT YEAR in documentation searches.**

### Rule 2: YOU CANNOT SEE THE UI

**You are blind to rendered output.** You MUST use MCP visual testing.

```
What you know:              Reality:
✓ Code compiled             ✗ Blank white screen
✓ No TypeScript errors      ✗ React error boundary
✓ Tests passed              ✗ Component doesn't render
```

**NEVER mark a UI feature as complete without MCP screenshot verification.**

### Rule 3: INSTALL DEPENDENCIES VIA COMMANDS

**NEVER manually edit dependency files to add packages.**

```bash
# CORRECT
bun add react
bun add @react-three/fiber

# WRONG - DO NOT DO THIS
# Manually editing package.json: "react": "^18.0.0"
```

Why: Package managers resolve correct versions and peer dependencies.

### Rule 4: RESEARCH BEFORE GUESSING

**When stuck, STOP and research.** Don't try random solutions.

See the STOP-SEARCH-UNDERSTAND-APPLY method below.

### Rule 5: SEARCH FOR UPDATED DOCUMENTATION

**Your training data is stale.** For complex configurations:

```bash
# Always search with current year
WebSearch: "[technology] [topic] documentation 2025"
WebFetch: [official docs URL]
```

**Do NOT rely on training data for:**
- Library configurations
- API patterns
- Framework setup
- Plugin systems

### Rule 6: VERIFY COMMANDS BEFORE RUNNING

**NEVER assume a command will work.** Before running ANY command:

```bash
# WRONG - Running blindly
bun run dev  # ← May fail if monorepo filter is wrong

# CORRECT - Verify first
cat package.json | grep -A10 '"scripts"'  # Check what scripts exist
./init.sh --no-server                      # Verify environment
bun run dev                                 # Then run
```

**If a command fails:**
1. **READ the error message** - Don't retry blindly
2. **Check the configuration** - Is the command correct for this project?
3. **Try the direct approach** - `cd apps/system && bun run dev`
4. **Document the solution** - Update relevant docs so future sessions don't repeat the error

**Common monorepo gotchas:**
- Root `package.json` scripts may use filters that don't match package names
- Always check `apps/*/package.json` for the actual package name
- When in doubt, run commands directly in the package folder

---

## MCP VISUAL TESTING (MANDATORY)

### Why This is Required

You cannot see the app you're building. Without visual testing, you will:
- Mark blank screens as "working"
- Miss CSS/layout issues
- Overlook runtime errors visible only in UI

### Testing Protocol: Playwright MCP

Use **Playwright MCP** for visual tests. Runs headless and **does not steal focus** from the user's window.

#### Step 1: Navigate to the page

```
mcp__playwright__browser_navigate url="file:///full/path/to/file.html"
```

Or for local server:
```
mcp__playwright__browser_navigate url="http://localhost:4321"
```

#### Step 2: Check console (JS errors)

```
mcp__playwright__browser_console_messages level="error"
```

- Red errors → **FAIL** (investigate before continuing)
- Yellow warnings → Evaluate (e.g., "Tailwind CDN production warning" is OK)

#### Step 3: Capture screenshot

```
mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot filename="screenshots/test-name.png"
```

For scrollable pages:
```
mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot fullPage=true filename="screenshots/test-full.png"
```

#### Step 4: Analyze visually

Check in the screenshot:
- [ ] Content visible? (not blank/empty)
- [ ] Layout correct? (elements positioned as expected)
- [ ] Colors correct? (matching design)
- [ ] No visual artifacts?

#### Step 5: Interactions (if needed)

```
mcp__playwright__browser_click element="button X" ref="e123"
mcp__playwright__browser_type element="field Y" ref="e456" text="value"
```

#### Step 6: Close browser at the end

```
mcp__playwright__browser_close
```

### Approval Criteria

| Criterion | PASS | FAIL |
|-----------|------|------|
| Console without errors | ✅ | ❌ Red errors |
| Content rendered | ✅ | ❌ Blank/empty screen |
| Layout correct | ✅ | ❌ Misaligned elements |
| JS executed | ✅ | ❌ Dynamic content missing |

### Final Decision

```
Visual OK + Console clean    → passes: true
Visual OK + Console warnings → Evaluate (may pass)
Visual OK + Console errors   → FAIL (fix it)
Visual broken                → FAIL (fix it)
```

### Playwright MCP Tools

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `mcp__playwright__browser_navigate` | Open page (file:// or http://) |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot` | Capture viewport or fullPage |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_snapshot` | Accessibility tree (debug) |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_console_messages` | View JS errors/warnings |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_click` | Click on element |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_type` | Type in field |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_close` | Close browser |


---

## ERROR HANDLING: STOP-SEARCH-UNDERSTAND-APPLY

### Classify the Error First

| Error Type | Action |
|------------|--------|
| Typo, syntax, obvious | Fix directly |
| Config, runtime, library, unknown | **RESEARCH FIRST** |

### The Method

```
1. STOP
   - Recognize you're stuck
   - Do NOT try random solutions

2. SEARCH
   - WebSearch: "[error] + [tech] + [CURRENT YEAR]"
   - WebFetch: Official documentation
   - Include current year, not training year

3. UNDERSTAND
   - Read results carefully
   - Identify ROOT CAUSE
   - Know WHY before fixing

4. APPLY
   - Implement correct solution
   - From documentation, not guessing

5. DOCUMENT
   - Update relevant docs if this is a recurring issue
```

---

## Project Structure

```
system/
├── apps/
│   └── canvas/           # Production app (Vite + React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS)
│       ├── src/
│       │   ├── main.tsx          # Entry point
│       │   ├── App.tsx           # Main component
│       │   ├── components/       # BlueprintJS UI components
│       │   ├── canvas/           # R3F rendering
│       │   │   ├── InfiniteCanvas.tsx
│       │   │   ├── CameraController.tsx
│       │   │   └── layers/       # Macro, Meso, Micro
│       │   ├── data/             # Types and generator
│       │   └── hooks/            # useViewport, etc.
│       ├── index.html
│       ├── vite.config.ts
│       └── package.json
├── packages/
│   └── toolkit/          # State machine, utilities (React 18 || 19)
├── design/
│   └── reference/        # POC and visual references
│       └── semantic-zoom-poc.html
├── docs/                 # Domain documentation
├── CLAUDE.md             # This file (project rules)
├── claude-progress.txt   # Session history
├── features.json         # Feature requirements (source of truth)
└── agent_docs/           # Architecture, testing, conventions
```

## Key Files

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `apps/canvas/src/main.tsx` | Production app entry |
| `apps/canvas/src/App.tsx` | Main React component |
| `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` | R3F canvas |
| `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/*.tsx` | LOD rendering layers |
| `apps/canvas/src/data/generator.ts` | Mock data generation |
| `packages/toolkit/` | Shared state machine |
| `design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html` | POC (visual/algo reference) |

## LOD System (Level of Detail)

The system implements three rendering layers based on camera zoom:

- **Macro (zoom < 0.5):** Procedural shader heatmap - 1 draw call
- **Meso (0.5 ≤ zoom ≤ 2.0):** InstancedMesh with 60k instances
- **Micro (zoom > 2.0):** HTML overlay with Line2 dependency curves

## Current State

Read `claude-progress.txt` for session history and `features.json` for requirements status.

## Documentation

### Domain Documentation (`docs/`)
- @docs/README.md - Documentation index
- @docs/DOMAIN.md - Fleet, maintenance types, entities
- @docs/REQUIREMENTS.md - Business requirements (from RFQ)
- @docs/UX_SPECIFICATION.md - UX design and interactions
- @docs/RESEARCH.md - Industry research and best practices

### Technical Documentation (`agent_docs/`)
- @agent_docs/architecture.md - Architecture details
- @agent_docs/testing.md - Testing protocol
- @agent_docs/conventions.md - Code conventions
- @agent_docs/ai-limitations.md - AI limitations and mitigations

### Work Protocol

```

```

### Self-Correction Protocol

2. **ANALYZE:** Why was local documentation unclear?
3. **FIX:** Update CLAUDE.md or hooks to improve clarity
4. **DOCUMENT:** Record the fix in claude-progress.txt

### Harness Files

| File                  | Purpose              | When to Read       |
|-----------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| `claude-progress.txt` | Session history      | **ALWAYS first**   |
| `features.json`       | Features with status | After progress.txt |
| `CLAUDE.md`           | Project rules        | Reference          |

---

## KISS Philosophy

**KISS is NOT "do the minimum". It IS "don't add complexity that blocks progress".**

### What KISS means (examples)

| ✅ DO                                                         | ❌ DON'T                                                          |
|--------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Add polish, debug tools, UX refinements that accelerate work | Spend days on infrastructure before having something presentable |
| Use best practices that prevent future problems              | Add abstractions for hypothetical future needs                   |
| Preserve knowledge/tips for when they become relevant        | Ignore proven patterns just because "not needed yet"             |

### When to Increase Complexity (examples)

| ✅ Increase complexity when...     | ❌ Do NOT increase when... |
|-----------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Current approach blocks a feature | "It would be cleaner"     |
| Performance is unacceptable       | "Best practices say so"   |
| Code duplication becomes painful  | Premature optimization    |

### Example: POC-first approach

### Tech Stack

|---------------|---------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Vite**      | 5.x     | Build tool and dev server                 |
| **React**     | 18.3.1  | UI framework (required for BlueprintJS 6) |
| **BlueprintJS** | 6.1.0 | UI component library                      |
| **R3F**       | 8.x     | React Three Fiber for WebGL               |
| **drei**      | 9.x     | R3F helpers and components                |
| **Three.js**  | 0.170.x | 3D rendering engine                       |

### Features

| Feature       | Description                                         | LOD Level |
|---------------|-----------------------------------------------------|-----------|
| **Pan**       | Drag to navigate through time (X) and trains (Y)    | All       |
| **Zoom**      | Scroll to zoom in/out, zoom centers on cursor       | All       |
| **Macro View** | Heatmap showing task density (yellow→orange→red)   | < 0.5x    |
| **Meso View** | DNA-like task strips with colored blocks            | 0.5x - 2x |
| **Micro View** | Detailed task cards with labels and grid           | > 2x      |
| **Sidebar**   | Train IDs synced with vertical pan (BlueprintJS Card) | All     |
| **Timeline**  | Date labels with adaptive granularity               | All       |

### Roadmap

See `features.json` for complete feature list. Key phases:

```
✅ F001-F015:  POC validation (archived)
🔄 F101-F103:  Foundation - Vite + React 18 + BlueprintJS + R3F ← CURRENT
⏳ F104-F107:  Core rendering - Data, MesoLayer, pan/zoom, LOD
⏳ F108-F110:  All LOD layers - Macro, Micro, transitions
⏳ F111-F114:  UI integration - Sidebar, timeline, controls
⏳ F115-F117:  Polish - Instructions, performance, visual parity
```
